[202]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 闲斋曰 - Formal opening meaning 'Idle Studio says' or 'Idle Studio states.'
  • 淫狐 - Metaphorical reference to a fox with excessive or immoral behavior.
  • 报冤 - Concept from Chinese folklore involving supernatural elements where a wrong is avenged.
  • 好名之甚者矣 - Phrase meaning 'one who loves reputation excessively.'
  • 实之宾 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the guest of reality,' suggesting that a name or reputation is secondary to the actual nature of something.
  • 无趣 - Term meaning 'boring' or 'lacking in interest,' often used to describe a person or situation.
  • 老羞成怒 - Compound term meaning 'old shame turning into anger,' describing a person who becomes angry due to past embarrassment or humiliation.
  • 附学 - Term meaning 'to join in learning' or 'to participate in studies,' often in a secondary or supplementary capacity.
  • 半岁余矣 - Term meaning 'more than half a year,' indicating a significant period of time.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 淫狐 - Metaphorical reference to a fox with excessive or immoral behavior, often used in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 报冤 - Concept from Chinese folklore and literature, often involving supernatural elements where a wrong is avenged.
  • 好名 - Cultural value in Chinese society, where reputation and honor are highly prized, but excessive concern for reputation can be seen as a flaw.
  • 实之宾 - Philosophical concept suggesting that a name or reputation is secondary to the actual nature of something, reflecting a deeper understanding of reality.
  • 无趣 - Cultural term used to describe a person or situation that lacks interest or excitement, often in a negative context.
  • 老羞成怒 - Common expression in Chinese culture, describing a person who becomes angry due to past embarrassment or humiliation.
  • 贵筑蔡孝廉 - Respected scholar from Guizhou, China, known for his erudition and contributions to literature and scholarship.
  • 兰岩 - Pen name or alias, often used by scholars and writers to sign their works.
  • 胡辉岸 - Scholar or writer, possibly a contemporary of the others mentioned, known for his contributions to literature and commentary.

Proper Nouns

  • 闲斋 - Pen name or alias, possibly used by a scholar or writer.
  • 兰岩 - Pen name or alias, possibly used by a scholar or writer.
  • 胡辉岸 - Scholar or writer, possibly a contemporary of the others mentioned.
  • 贵筑蔡孝廉 - Respected scholar from Guizhou, China, known for his erudition.
  • 褚十二 - Person mentioned in the narrative, likely a friend or acquaintance of the scholar.
  • 顾明经 - Teacher or mentor, possibly a respected scholar.
  • 巴蜀 - Region in southwestern China, historically known for its cultural and political significance.
  • 临邛 - City in Sichuan, known for its historical and cultural importance.
  • 罗氏 - Prominent family in the region, known for their wealth and status.
  • 秦生 - Person mentioned in the narrative, likely a friend or acquaintance of褚十二.

[203]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 時當秋月 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'it was during the autumn moon,' setting the temporal and atmospheric scene.
  • 值羅次子畢婚 - Means 'when Luo's second son had his wedding,' indicating a specific event.
  • 顧連日困於酒食 - Means 'but he had been exhausted by continuous feasting and drinking,' describing the state of the character.
  • 秦亦理事甚忙 - Means 'Qin was also very busy with his affairs,' providing additional context about another character.
  • 褚獨步軒中 - Means 'Zhu walked alone in the hall,' setting the scene for the character's actions.
  • 深苦岑寂 - Means 'deeply felt the loneliness and emptiness,' expressing the character's emotional state.
  • 抽書破悶 - Means 'pulled out a book to relieve boredom,' describing the action taken to alleviate the feeling of loneliness.
  • 漏二下 - Means 'it was two hours after midnight,' providing a precise time reference.
  • 秦生攜酒盒來與褚小酌 - Means 'Qin brought a wine box to share a small drink with Zhu,' setting the scene for the interaction between the two characters.
  • 逐日碌碌 - Means 'every day busy with trivial matters,' describing a lifestyle.
  • 未遑晤對 - Means 'had no time to meet and talk,' explaining why they hadn't seen each other.
  • 今宵稍暇 - Means 'tonight we have a little free time,' providing a reason for their meeting.
  • 聊具杯酒 - Means 'let's have a few cups of wine,' suggesting a casual and friendly gathering.
  • 與子談心 - Means 'to talk to you heart-to-heart,' indicating the purpose of their meeting.
  • 屏童僕 - Means 'to dismiss the servants,' creating a private setting.
  • 扃園門 - Means 'to lock the garden gate,' ensuring privacy.
  • 挑燈細酌 - Means 'to light the lamp and drink slowly,' setting the atmosphere for their conversation.
  • 頗極歡暢 - Means 'we were very happy and relaxed,' describing their mood.
  • 浮白 - A term meaning 'toasting,' used in the context of drinking.
  • 人生行樂耳 - Means 'life is about enjoying oneself,' expressing a philosophical view.
  • 偃鼠飲河 - A metaphor meaning 'a mouse drinking from the Yellow River,' symbolizing greed or insatiable desire.
  • 徒飲豈足以為樂 - Means 'just drinking is not enough for enjoyment,' expressing the idea that simple pleasures are insufficient.
  • 予有一妙人 - Means 'I have a wonderful person,' introducing a new character.
  • 兄如見之，當思老於是鄉矣 - Means 'if you see her, you will think I am old in this place,' expressing a sense of regret or longing.
  • 問為何如人 - Means 'ask what kind of person she is,' seeking more information.
  • 秦支吾不以實告 - Means 'Qin hesitated and did not tell the truth,' indicating reluctance to share information.
  • 力叩之 - Means 'insisted on asking,' showing determination to get an answer.
  • 秦始小語曰 - Means 'Qin finally whispered,' indicating a change in tone and the beginning of a secret revelation.
  • 誠為狐 - Means 'if she is indeed a fox,' expressing a possibility.
  • 非鬼也 - Means 'not a ghost,' clarifying the nature of the person.
  • 狐而色比宓妃 - Means 'a fox with a beauty comparable to that of the goddess of the moon,' describing the person's appearance.
  • 才同謝女 - Means 'talent equal to that of Xie Daoyun,' praising the person's intelligence or artistic ability.
  • 何不可親昵之有 - Means 'how could there be any reason not to be close to her,' expressing a positive attitude towards the person.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秋月 - Refers to the autumn moon, a common poetic motif in Chinese literature, often associated with beauty and tranquility.
  • 畢婚 - Refers to a wedding ceremony, a significant social event in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 酒食 - Refers to drinking and feasting, common social activities in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 秦生 - A name, likely referring to a historical or fictional character.
  • 褚 - A name, likely referring to a historical or fictional character.
  • 漏二下 - Refers to a traditional Chinese timekeeping device, the water clock, indicating a specific time.
  • 偃鼠飲河 - A metaphor from Chinese folklore, symbolizing greed or insatiable desire.
  • 宓妃 - Refers to the goddess of the moon, a figure from Chinese mythology.
  • 謝女 - Refers to Xie Daoyun, a famous poet from the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for her literary talent.

Proper Nouns

  • 羅次子 - Refers to Luo's second son, a character in the story.
  • 秦生 - Refers to Qin, a character in the story.
  • 褚 - Refers to Zhu, a character in the story.
  • 宓妃 - Refers to the goddess of the moon, a figure from Chinese mythology.
  • 謝女 - Refers to Xie Daoyun, a famous poet from the Eastern Jin Dynasty.

[204]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 血氣未定 - Refers to the idea that the blood and qi (vital energy) are not yet stable, indicating youth and inexperience.
  • 面嫩 - A term used to describe someone who is young and inexperienced, often with a hint of naivety.
  • 良心現 - A term indicating that someone's true nature or good conscience is revealed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 褚 - A common surname, likely referring to a historical or fictional character.
  • 秦 - Another common surname, likely referring to a historical or fictional character.
  • 怜姐 - A nickname for a woman, possibly a courtesan or a woman of high social standing.
  • 書痴 - A term for a person who is overly fond of books, often with a negative connotation.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 岂似尔天良尽丧，毫不知羞耻哉！ - Rhetorical question expressing strong disapproval, meaning 'How can you have lost all sense of right and wrong, and not know shame at all?'
  • 二酸子 - Colloquial term of endearment or affection, possibly meaning 'two little snots' or 'two little guys.'
  • 扪结 - Term that is difficult to translate directly, possibly referring to a specific action or object, but its exact meaning is unclear without more context.
  • 饱嗉 - Term that is difficult to translate directly, possibly referring to a specific state or condition, but its exact meaning is unclear without more context.
  • 诚旅店蜰虫 - Metaphorical expression comparing someone to a parasite in a hotel, suggesting someone who is a burden or a nuisance.
  • 谋食客 - Means 'to seek food for guests,' suggesting a parasitic or opportunistic behavior.
  • 羊桃 - Type of fruit, possibly a specific variety of peach or another fruit, but its exact identity is uncertain.
  • 珍馔芳醪 - Refers to fine food and fragrant wine, indicating high-quality provisions.
  • 撞破迷楼 - Metaphorical expression suggesting breaking through a maze or confusion, possibly referring to achieving a goal or overcoming an obstacle.
  • 千叶桃花，早荣早落，华而不实 - Metaphor comparing someone to a thousand-petaled peach blossom that blooms and falls early, suggesting someone who is beautiful but lacks substance.
  • 行乐及时 - Means 'to enjoy pleasure in due time,' suggesting the importance of seizing opportunities.
  • 株待 - Means 'to wait for a tree to grow,' suggesting a passive or unproductive approach.
  • 萍水相遭 - Metaphor for meeting by chance, like floating on water.
  • 逢场作戏 - Means 'to act on the spur of the moment,' suggesting a casual or spontaneous approach to life.
  • 何足介意 - Means 'not worth worrying about,' suggesting that something is trivial or insignificant.
  • 少时新妇之事 - Refers to matters related to a newly married wife, possibly including marital duties or responsibilities.
  • 罗家郎 - Refers to a man named Luo, possibly a character in the story.
  • 行当自见耳 - Means 'you will see for yourself,' suggesting that the truth will become clear in time.

[206]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有顷 - Temporal phrase meaning 'after a while' or 'soon after.'
  • 蓦闻 - Means 'suddenly heard' or 'heard unexpectedly.'
  • 人声鼎沸 - Vivid metaphor meaning 'the sound of people was like boiling water,' describing a noisy crowd.
  • 延及 - Means 'spread to' or 'extended to.'
  • 蚁聚蜂屯 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'gathered like ants and bees,' describing a large crowd.
  • 家口数十人 - Refers to 'dozens of family members.'
  • 身无寸缕 - Means 'not a single thread of clothing,' describing someone who is completely naked.
  • 股栗 - Means 'shivering with fear.'
  • 纤毫毕见 - Means 'every detail is visible,' describing how clearly something can be seen.
  • 二生不能正视 - Means 'the two young men could not look directly at her.'
  • 解罗衣裹之 - Means 'took off her robe and wrapped her in it.'
  • 掖归别室 - Means 'helped her back to a separate room.'
  • 接踵来慰 - Means 'came one after another to comfort her.'
  • 抢攘间已失女之所在 - Means 'in the chaos, the girl was lost.'
  • 唯秦褚二生知之 - Means 'only the two young men from Qin and Chu knew about it.'
  • 缄密不言 - Means 'kept it secret and did not speak of it.'
  • 无夕不与晤对 - Means 'met with her every night.'
  • 相得甚欢 - Means 'got along very well and were very happy together.'
  • 然终未及乱 - Means 'but they never went too far.'
  • 腼腆 - Means 'bashful' or 'shy.'
  • 贞操 - Means 'chastity' or 'virtue.'
  • 韦崟之与任氏 - Refers to a famous couple in Chinese literature known for their virtuous relationship.
  • 从其父归成都 - Means 'followed his father back to Chengdu.'
  • 泣别 - Means 'parted with tears.'
  • 顾死 - Means 'died suddenly.'
  • 扶枢归乡 - Means 'carried the coffin back to his hometown.'
  • 未及获隽 - Means 'did not have time to achieve success.'
  • 次岁成进士 - Means 'the next year, he passed the imperial examination.'
  • 工部观政 - Means 'served as an official in the Ministry of Works.'
  • 未娶而卒 - Means 'died unmarried.'
  • 年甫二十有四云 - Means 'he was just twenty-four years old.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庖人失火 - Refers to a cook starting a fire, which was a common household accident in ancient times.
  • 坊正官军 - Refers to the local officials and soldiers who came to extinguish the fire.
  • 亲邻渐集 - Describes the gathering of family and neighbors, reflecting the communal nature of ancient Chinese society.
  • 洞房 - Refers to the bridal chamber, often used to describe a private or intimate space.
  • 罗氏子 - Refers to a young man from the Luo family, likely a noble or wealthy family.
  • 新妇 - Refers to a newly married woman.
  • 秦褚二生 - Refers to two young men from the Qin and Chu families, likely noble or scholarly families.

Proper Nouns

  • 庖人 - The cook.
  • 坊正 - Local officials.
  • 洞房 - The bridal chamber.
  • 罗氏子 - A young man from the Luo family.
  • 新妇 - A newly married woman.
  • 秦褚二生 - Two young men from the Qin and Chu families.
  • 韦崟 - A famous figure from Chinese literature.
  • 任氏 - A famous figure from Chinese literature.
  • 成都 - The city of Chengdu.
  • 工部 - The Ministry of Works.

[207]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 闲斋曰 - Attribution indicating the following text is from someone known as '闲斋' (Xianzai).
  • 酸子 - Refers to a person who is overly suspicious or jealous, often used in a derogatory sense.
  • 多妒者 - Means 'those who are often jealous.'
  • 穷其故无他 - A phrase meaning 'to exhaust the cause without any other reason.'
  • 闭户守妻子若将终身 - Means 'to close the door and guard one's wife and children as if they will stay forever.'
  • 本无远大志耳 - Means 'originally without any grand ambitions.'
  • 贞狐与褚 - Refers to a female fox and a man named Chu, likely a reference to a specific story or character.
  • 不过话言形色之间涉于狎亵 - Means 'nothing more than words and expressions that involve familiarity or intimacy.'
  • 书痴而形诸色 - Means 'a bookworm who shows it in their appearance.'
  • 何所见之小也 - Means 'how small is their vision.'
  • 兰岩曰 - Another attribution, this time to someone known as '兰岩' (Lanyan).
  • 守身贞 - Means 'to maintain one's chastity.'
  • 见理明 - Means 'to understand principles clearly.'
  • 出词雅 - Means 'to speak or write in an elegant manner.'
  • 比狐不多得 - Means 'such a fox is not easily found.'
  • 鞠慕周最善说狐 - Means 'Ju Mu Zhou is the best at telling stories about foxes.'
  • 不能悉记 - Means 'cannot fully remember.'
  • 其有奇者 - Means 'those that are particularly interesting.'
  • 足发一大噱 - Means 'enough to cause a big laugh.'
  • 言其客关中时 - Means 'speaking of when he was a guest in Guanzhong.'
  • 因事之扶风 - Means 'because of some matter in Fufeng.'
  • 所识有丁孝廉者 - Means 'there was a scholar named Ding who was known to him.'
  • 年近四十 - Means 'nearly forty years old.'
  • 断三弦 - Refers to a type of musical instrument, likely a three-stringed lute.
  • 子女皆幼 - Means 'his children were all young.'
  • 号啼绕膝 - Means 'cries and wails around his knees.'
  • 不耐鳏居 - Means 'unable to bear being a widower.'
  • 仍谋胶续 - Means 'still planning to remarry.'
  • 屡乖所愿 - Means 'repeatedly failing to achieve his wishes.'
  • 丁素究心神仙之事 - Means 'Ding had always been interested in immortality and spiritual matters.'
  • 精于导引 - Means 'skilled in guiding and leading (practices).'
  • 每澄心枯坐 - Means 'often meditating in a state of emptiness.'
  • 吐故纳新 - Means 'to expel old and inhale new (referring to breathing exercises).'
  • 则见一黑狐对面蹲踞 - Means 'then he saw a black fox sitting opposite him.'
  • 瞠目相向 - Means 'staring at each other.'
  • 丁叱之 - Means 'Ding scolded it.'
  • 即刻奔逸 - Means 'immediately fled away.'
  • 如此者屡矣 - Means 'this happened many times.'
  • 亦习不为怪 - Means 'he also got used to it and no longer found it strange.'
  • 一夜方坐 - Means 'one night, he was sitting.'
  • 觉有人登榻 - Means 'he felt someone climb onto the bed.'
  • 与己并肩坐 - Means 'sitting next to him.'
  • 衣香袭人 - Means 'the fragrance of her clothes was overwhelming.'
  • 丁自念此皆妄虑之招 - Means 'Ding thought to himself that all this was the result of his own delusions.'
  • 心不动则魔何由生 - Means 'if the mind does not move, how can demons arise?'
  • 任之可也 - Means 'it can be left to its own devices.'
  • 于是垂目息心 - Means 'so he closed his eyes and calmed his mind.'
  • 凝然不动 - Means 'remaining motionless.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 闲斋 - Likely a pseudonym or nickname of the author or commentator, common in classical Chinese literature.
  • 兰岩 - Likely a pseudonym or nickname of the author or commentator, common in classical Chinese literature.
  • 酸子 - A term used to describe someone who is overly suspicious or jealous, often used in a derogatory sense.
  • 贞狐 - Refers to a female fox, often used in Chinese folklore and literature to symbolize various qualities, including cunning and beauty.
  • 褚 - A proper name, likely referring to a specific character in a story or historical context.
  • 关中 - Refers to the Guanzhong region, a historical area in central China.
  • 扶风 - A place name, likely referring to Fufeng, a county in Shaanxi Province.
  • 丁孝廉 - A scholar named Ding, likely a reference to a specific historical figure or fictional character.
  • 导引 - A traditional Chinese practice involving breathing exercises and meditation, often associated with Daoist practices.

[208]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既而 - Classical conjunction meaning 'after a while' or 'shortly afterward.'
  • 以颊偎腮 - Phrase describing the act of leaning one's cheek against another's cheek, often used to depict intimacy or affection.
  • 寻以口亲吻 - 'Soon after, with the mouth, kissed' — a poetic or archaic way of describing a kiss.
  • 粉香脂腻 - Refers to the fragrance of cosmetics and the smoothness of face cream; a poetic way of describing the beauty of a woman’s face.
  • 肌滑如脂 - 'Skin smooth as fat' — a metaphor for soft, smooth skin.
  • 不能复耐 - 'Could no longer endure' or 'could not bear it any longer.'
  • 张目瞩之 - 'Opened his eyes and looked at her' — a dramatic way of emphasizing attention or surprise.
  • 二八丽人 - Classical way of referring to a woman of 16 years (eighteen in Chinese counting), i.e., a young and beautiful woman.
  • 光采耀目 - 'Radiant beauty that dazzles the eyes' — a poetic description of someone’s brilliance or beauty.
  • 睨丁而笑 - 'Glanced at Ding and smiled' — a phrase that conveys a teasing or flirtatious look.
  • 吾固知汝为向日狐 - 'I have always known you to be the fox from before' — a reference to a previous encounter with a fox spirit.
  • 奈何扰人功课 - 'Why do you disturb my studies?' — a rhetorical question expressing frustration.
  • 可速去 - 'You should go quickly' — a direct command.
  • 否则惹老拳 - 'Otherwise, I’ll give you a good beating' — '老拳' is a colloquial term for a strong punch or beating.
  • 非善知识矣 - 'You are not a good companion' — a judgment on the fox’s behavior.
  • 犹掩口嗤嗤 - 'Still covering her mouth and giggling' — a description of continued laughter or amusement.
  • 俄延不去 - 'Delayed her departure' — indicating that the woman lingered or delayed leaving.
  • 躁甚 - 'Very impatient' — a description of emotional agitation.
  • 以足蹴之 - 'Struck her with his foot' — a physical act of pushing or kicking.
  • 颠堕床下 - 'Fell off the bed' — a result of being kicked.
  • 旋即起立 - 'Rose up immediately' — showing quick recovery.
  • 忿忿整衣 - 'Angrily adjusted her clothes' — an expression of indignation or anger.
  • 鲁莽如此 - 'So reckless' — a criticism of behavior.
  • 岂复读书人行径耶 - 'How could this be the behavior of a scholar?' — a rhetorical question expressing disapproval.
  • 儿去不复再来 - 'I will go and not return again' — a declaration of leaving permanently.
  • 汝其勿悔 - 'You should not regret it' — a warning or caution.
  • 鞠拱而谢 - 'Bowed and thanked' — a formal gesture of gratitude.
  • 深感厚谊 - 'Deeply grateful for your kindness' — an expression of sincere appreciation.
  • 敢云悔乎 - 'How could I dare say I regret it?' — a rhetorical question expressing no regret.
  • 从此虽焚香叩头，祈我再至，恐亦不能矣 - 'Even if you burned incense and knelt in prayer to me to come again, I fear it would not be possible' — a poetic and dramatic way of expressing finality.
  • 永不敢启动矣 - 'I will never dare to start this again' — a definitive statement of resolve.
  • 不顾而去 - 'Without looking back, she left' — a dramatic description of departure.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐 - In Chinese folklore, foxes are often depicted as shape-shifting spirits (fox spirits or *huli jing*), capable of taking human form and often associated with seduction or mischief. The reference to a 'fox' here implies that the woman is a supernatural being or spirit in human form.
  • 功课 - A classical term for study or learning, often used in the context of Confucian education.
  • 老拳 - A colloquial and somewhat vulgar term for a strong punch or beating, used to convey a threat or warning.
  • 善知识 - A Buddhist and Confucian term for a virtuous or good friend or associate.
  • 焚香叩头 - A traditional act of worship or supplication, often performed in religious or ritual contexts.

  • The discussion among the nuns and the suggestion to kill Mingxin '阴与三尼议，闵生狼狈若此，不如杀之，以泯其迹' must be read as a single, dramatic moment.
  • The reaction of Jingchu and her attempt to stop the others '景初大骇，急止之曰' must be read as a single, urgent action.
  • The actions of Jingchu in comforting and advising Mingxin '乃亟入室，抚闵慰藉之，戒其珍摄，无致萎顿' must be read as a series of compassionate acts.
  • The change in the behavior of the nuns and the increased care provided by Jingchu '自此诸尼，悉不复来，旦夕所需，皆景初殷勤伺奉' must be read as a single, continuous narrative.
  • Mingxin's gratitude and his constant longing for home '闵颇感之，思家之念，无刻不迫' must be read as a single, emotional state.
  • The religious practices and the physical setting of the room '室中旧奉观音一龛，朝夕拜祷，求脱陷井' must be read as a single, cohesive scene.
  • The discovery of the mantra, the practice of recitation, and the eventual mastery '又检案头经卷，得观音咒，诚心持诵之，日以数千遍，久之，睡梦成诵' must be read as a single, continuous process.

Proper Nouns

  • 明心 - A character name.
  • 三尼 - Refers to three nuns.
  • 景初 - A character name.
  • 观音 - Refers to Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of compassion.
  • 观音咒 - Refers to a Buddhist mantra associated with Guanyin.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有人呼名 - Literally 'someone called out a name,' indicating a supernatural summons.
  • 惊视之 - Literally 'startled and looked at it,' describing a sudden reaction.
  • 速下床 - Literally 'hurry down from the bed,' an urgent command.
  • 我送汝归 - Literally 'I will take you back,' a promise of assistance.
  • 迟则误乃事矣 - Literally 'if delayed, it will delay your matter,' emphasizing urgency.
  • 披衣跣足而走 - Literally 'put on clothes and ran barefoot,' describing hurried action.
  • 以手拂户 - Literally 'waved a hand at the door,' a magical gesture.
  • 门自辟 - Literally 'the door opened by itself,' indicating supernatural intervention.
  • 尾而随之 - Literally 'followed behind,' describing the action of following.
  • 身有白光如月 - Literally 'had a white light like the moon,' describing the supernatural appearance.
  • 到处映彻如昼 - Literally 'everywhere illuminated like daytime,' emphasizing the brightness.
  • 一路行复道中 - Literally 'traveled along a covered walkway,' describing the setting.
  • 两壁高峻，如城垣 - Literally 'two high walls, like city walls,' describing the environment.
  • 历数重门 - Literally 'passed through many doors,' indicating a journey through multiple barriers.
  • 媪至辄开 - Literally 'the woman arrived and the doors opened,' emphasizing ease of passage.
  • 即从此出，勿走回头路 - Literally 'just go out from here, do not take a backward path,' a warning.
  • 始悟为大士化身 - Literally 'finally realized it was the Bodhisattva's incarnation,' indicating spiritual insight.
  • 默诵宝号不绝 - Literally 'silently recited the Buddha's name without interruption,' describing continuous devotion.
  • 踉跄奔数里 - Literally 'staggered and ran several miles,' describing hurried movement.
  • 约去庵已远 - Literally 'estimated to be far from the hermitage,' indicating distance.
  • 仰视星转月斜 - Literally 'looked up and saw the stars moving and the moon slanting,' describing the time of night.
  • 可四更将半 - Literally 'it was nearly the middle of the fourth watch,' indicating the time.
  • 遂蹲身一土阜下憩焉 - Literally 'then sat down on a small hill to rest,' describing the action of resting.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大士 - Refers to a Bodhisattva, a being in Buddhist tradition who has achieved enlightenment but chooses to remain in the world to help others.
  • 宝号 - Refers to the Buddha's name, often recited in Buddhist practice for spiritual benefit.
  • 庵 - Refers to a hermitage or small temple, often associated with Buddhist monks or nuns.
  • 四更 - Refers to the fourth watch of the night, a traditional Chinese timekeeping system.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一陰一陽之謂道 - Classical phrase from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), meaning 'the interplay of yin and yang is called the Dao.'
  • 夫唱婦隨 - Traditional saying meaning 'the husband leads and the wife follows,' reflecting Confucian ideals of marital harmony.
  • 三代維隆 - 'The Three Dynasties were in their prime,' referring to the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties, idealized as times of moral and political excellence.
  • 屏異端於域外 - 'Excluding heretical doctrines from the realm,' used to justify the suppression of non-Confucian beliefs.
  • 二南攸美 - 'The Two Nans are beautiful,' referring to the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry)’s 'Nan' poems, considered elegant and refined.
  • 敦雅化於房中 - 'Promoting refined culture within the inner chambers,' referring to the moral and cultural education of women in the home.
  • 怨女旷夫 - 'Widowed women and bachelor men,' referring to those unmarried due to misfortune or loss.
  • 仁政最怜失偶 - 'Benevolent governance most pities those who have lost their partners,' indicating compassion for the socially disadvantaged.
  • 孤鸾寡凤 - A metaphor for lonely and unmatched couples, often used in poetry to express sorrow over broken relationships.
  • 诗人致慨离群 - 'Poets express sorrow over being isolated from the group,' referring to the emotional tone of classical poetry.
  • 佛国云遥 - 'The Buddha-land is far away,' a poetic way of saying the ideal Buddhist realm is distant or unattainable.
  • 空门不靖 - 'The monastic order is not at peace,' suggesting disorder or corruption within Buddhist institutions.
  • 西来大意 - 'The great meaning from the West,' referring to the original teachings of the Buddha, which came from India to China.
  • 日就披靡 - 'Day by day, it is falling into decline,' indicating a gradual weakening or collapse.
  • 东土众生 - 'The people of the East,' referring to the Chinese people.
  • 自为簧鼓 - 'They themselves become the flutes and drums,' a metaphor for being led astray by false teachings or self-deception.
  • 绀宇琳宫 - 'Purple-roofed halls and crystal palaces,' poetic terms for Buddhist monasteries.
  • 阇黎 - A transliteration of 'sādhu' or 'monk,' used in classical Chinese texts to refer to Buddhist monks.
  • 香台兰若 - 'Incense altars and fragrant hermitages,' poetic terms for monastic or religious sites.
  • 魔女群居 - 'Demons and women gather together,' suggesting the presence of evil or corrupting influences among the monastic community.
  • 任化裁固难缓于沙弥 - 'The task of transformation and guidance is certainly not easy for novice monks,' indicating the difficulty of spiritual guidance.
  • 开导宜先施诸愚妇 - 'Instruction should first be given to foolish women,' suggesting the author believes women are the primary targets for Buddhist teachings or reform.
  • 顾念伊剃度，亦有因缘 - 'Considering their ordination, there are also karmic connections,' indicating the women’s decision to become nuns is not entirely without reason or cause.
  • 或多病而误信星书 - 'Many were sick and mistakenly believed in star charts,' referring to people relying on astrology or superstition for medical or life decisions.
  • 父母忍心割舍 - 'Parents, with cruel hearts, cut them off,' indicating the emotional pain of parents sending their children to monastic life.
  • 或早寡而情伤破镜 - 'Or they were widowed early and their hearts were broken by the shattered mirror,' a metaphor for the sorrow of a broken marriage or loss of a spouse.
  • 闺门绝意修容 - 'The inner chambers gave up the intention to beautify,' suggesting the loss of hope or desire for a normal life or marriage.
  • 或失琴瑟之调 - 'Or they lost the harmony of the zither and lute,' a metaphor for the breakdown of marital harmony.
  • 逞小忿而乌云辄剪 - 'To vent a small anger, they cut the clouds of hair,' meaning they took drastic action (like shaving their heads) out of anger or frustration.
  • 或抱琵琶之恨 - 'Or they carried the sorrow of the pipa,' a metaphor for unfulfilled love or longing, referencing the classical story of Pipa (a musician).
  • 恐中弃而白发靡依 - 'Fearing that they would be abandoned and have no one to rely on in old age,' indicating fear of being left alone in life.
  • 礼金粟以向空门 - 'Offering gold and grains to enter the monastic life,' referring to the material offerings made to become a nun or monk.
  • 本图忏悔 - 'Originally intending to repent,' showing the initial motivation for entering monastic life.
  • 拥蒲团而课静室 - 'Holding the rush mat and meditating in the quiet chamber,' describing the daily life of a nun or monk.
  • 渐觉孤清 - 'Gradually feeling lonely and clear,' indicating the emotional isolation and spiritual clarity that comes with monastic life.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 一陰一陽之謂道 - Core concept from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), central to Chinese cosmology and philosophy.
  • 夫唱婦隨 - Confucian ideal of marital roles, reflecting traditional gender roles and social harmony.
  • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties, often idealized as the golden age of Chinese civilization.
  • 二南 - Refers to the 'Nan' poems in the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), considered to represent the elegance and refinement of southern Chinese culture.
  • 佛国 - A term for the ideal Buddhist realm, often used in Chinese Buddhist texts.
  • 西来大意 - The teachings of the Buddha, which came from the West (India) to China.
  • 东土 - A term for China, often used in Buddhist texts to distinguish it from the West.
  • 绀宇琳宫 - Poetic terms for Buddhist monasteries.
  • 阇黎 - A term for Buddhist monks, often used in classical Chinese texts.
  • 香台兰若 - Poetic terms for Buddhist temples or monasteries.
  • 魔女 - A term for female demons or corrupting influences, often used to critique the moral state of Buddhist monastic communities.
  • 沙弥 - A novice monk, a term used in Buddhist monastic traditions.
  • 剃度 - The act of shaving one’s head and becoming a monk or nun, a formal rite of entry into monastic life.
  • 星书 - Astrological or star-related texts, used in traditional Chinese medicine and fortune-telling.
  • 琴瑟 - The zither and lute, traditional Chinese musical instruments, used as a metaphor for marital harmony.
  • 琵琶 - A classical Chinese musical instrument, also used in poetry to symbolize unfulfilled love or sorrow.
  • 礼金粟 - Offering of money and grain, often used in religious or monastic contexts.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 暮鼓晨钟 - Refers to the evening and morning bells of a Buddhist temple, symbolizing the passage of time and the rhythm of life.
  • 翻出凄凉之响 - Literally 'turning out a sound of desolation,' but in context, it refers to the mournful or melancholic tone of the bells.
  • 春花秋月 - A poetic phrase meaning 'spring flowers and autumn moons,' symbolizing the beauty and transience of nature.
  • 暗生活泼之机 - Literally 'secretly harboring lively opportunities,' but in context, it suggests that even in the midst of desolation, there is potential for vitality or renewal.
  • 借托钵以延门 - Literally 'borrowing alms bowls to extend to the door,' referring to the act of begging for alms, which is a common practice among monks.
  • 每致桑间之约 - Literally 'often leading to agreements in the mulberry grove,' but in context, it refers to illicit or improper relationships.
  • 假安禅而闭户 - Literally 'pretending to meditate while closing the door,' suggesting hypocrisy or insincerity in religious practice.
  • 频来月下之敲 - Literally 'frequently coming to knock under the moon,' referring to the sound of a monk's staff striking the ground at night.
  • 阿鼻之罪孽难消 - Literally 'the sins of the Avīchi hell are hard to expiate,' referring to the most severe punishment in Buddhist cosmology.
  • 没齿而声名尽堕 - Literally 'until death, one's reputation is utterly ruined,' meaning that one's bad deeds will tarnish their reputation forever.
  • 爰为善计，莫如返本还原 - Literally 'to plan for good, nothing is better than returning to the original state,' suggesting that the best way to do good is to return to one's true nature.
  • 代作良图，须是改头换面 - Literally 'to make a good plan, one must change one's appearance,' meaning that significant change is necessary for improvement.
  • 壮者亟当择偶，幼者速使归宗 - Literally 'the strong should quickly choose a mate, the young should quickly return to their clan,' suggesting that marriage and family are important social obligations.
  • 纵或绕树无栖，自有缝裳之掺手 - Literally 'even if there is no place to rest under the tree, there are hands to mend the clothes,' meaning that help is always available.
  • 即使折心不转，何妨绣佛以明心 - Literally 'even if the heart is broken and cannot change, why not embroider a Buddha to clarify the heart,' suggesting that spiritual practice can help one find clarity and purpose.
  • 与其暗脱袈裟，渎汙三宝；曷若明摇环珮，讲究三从 - Literally 'rather than secretly removing the robe and desecrating the Three Jewels, why not openly wear the jade pendants and follow the Three Obediences,' meaning that it is better to be open and honest in one's actions.
  • 学簪花而舍拈花 - Literally 'learning to wear flowers but giving up the flower,' suggesting that one should not abandon the essence of something just because it is no longer fashionable.
  • 倩贝叶以充红叶 - Literally 'using the leaves of the bodhi tree to replace the red leaves,' meaning that one should not substitute something of lesser value for something more meaningful.
  • 明镜总非台，幸有温峤玉镜；赤绳堪系足，无须弥勒金绳 - Literally 'the bright mirror is not the stand, but luckily there is Wen Qiao's jade mirror; the red cord can tie the foot, no need for the Golden Rope of Maitreya,' suggesting that one should rely on practical solutions rather than relying on supernatural or divine intervention.
  • 苦海翻身，昏波臻岸 - Literally 'turning over in the sea of suffering, reaching the shore in the dark waves,' meaning that one can overcome suffering and find peace.
  • 是则宿愿恰成心愿，无情化作有情 - Literally 'this is how the long-held wish becomes the heart's desire, and the indifferent becomes compassionate,' suggesting that one's deepest desires can be fulfilled through compassion and kindness.
  • 甘露润菩提，始信因缘结果；春风吹柢树，欣看连理成枝 - Literally 'the sweet dew nourishes the bodhi tree, only then does one believe in the results of causes and conditions; the spring wind blows on the root tree, happily seeing the intertwined branches,' meaning that good deeds lead to positive outcomes and that harmony and unity can be achieved.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 暮鼓晨钟 - Refers to the traditional practice of ringing bells in Buddhist temples at dawn and dusk.
  • 阿鼻之罪孽难消 - Refers to the Avīchi hell, the most severe level of hell in Buddhist cosmology.
  • 温峤玉镜 - Refers to a famous story where Wen Qiao used a jade mirror to find a lost person, symbolizing wisdom and insight.
  • 赤绳 - Refers to the red cord that binds couples together in Chinese folklore, symbolizing marriage and fate.
  • 弥勒金绳 - Refers to the Golden Rope of Maitreya, a concept in Buddhism that suggests that one's fate is predetermined by the Buddha Maitreya.

  • This phrase criticizes the insincerity of monks who pretend to meditate but are actually engaging in other activities.

Proper Nouns

  • 阿鼻 - The most severe level of hell in Buddhist cosmology.
  • 温峤 - A famous general and statesman in Chinese history known for his wisdom and insight.
  • 弥勒 - The Buddha of the future, often associated with compassion and harmony.

  • 日暮抵大靖营 - Time-place description: 'At dusk, arrived at the camp of Dajing.' '抵' means 'arrive at' or 'reach,' and '营' is 'camp.'
  • 参戎马成龙见之愕然 - '参戎' is a title, possibly a military officer or assistant general; '马成龙' is the name of the person; '见之愕然' means 'saw it and was astonished.'
  • 苟山移，公等无噍类矣 - Hyperbolic warning: 'If the mountain were to move, you all would not have a single survivor.' '苟' is a conditional particle meaning 'if'; '山移' is 'the mountain moves'; '噍类' is a classical term meaning 'survivors' or 'living beings.'
  • 入秋则有之，至冬尤甚 - Standard formulaic expression in classical Chinese to indicate frequency and intensity: 'It occurs in autumn; it is especially so in winter.'
  • 今隆冬无足怪 - '隆冬' is 'deep winter' or 'severe winter'; '无足怪' is 'nothing to be surprised at.'
  • 所可虑者 - Classical phrase meaning 'what is to be feared' or 'what is concerning.'
  • 公与彼三十余人，从此胥成麻皮 - '胥成' is an archaic term meaning 'all become'; '麻皮' refers to a scarred or pitted skin condition. The phrase means 'you and the thirty or so of them will all become scarred.'
  • 年貌册又须另造矣 - '年貌册' is 'records of age and appearance'; '另造' is 'recreate' or 'recompile.' The full phrase suggests that official records of age and appearance will need to be rewritten.
  • 沉浮宦海中 - Metaphor meaning 'to rise and fall in the sea of officialdom' – i.e., to have a fluctuating career in government service.
  • 历有年所 - '历有年所' means 'has been for many years.'
  • 冲锋破敌 - 'to charge into battle and break through the enemy.'
  • 几历危途 - 'nearly encountered many dangerous paths' or 'nearly perished many times.'
  • 行年五十矣 - 'At the age of fifty.'
  • 狞飚 - Rare term meaning 'fierce gale' or 'violent wind.'
  • 不特未见，亦未之闻 - Double negative construction meaning 'not only have I not seen it, but I have not even heard of it.'
  • 面多疤痕 - 'Face full of scars.'
  • 额角左颊者尤巨 - 'The scar on the forehead and left cheek is especially large.'
  • 石子所嵌处也 - 'The place where a stone was embedded.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大靖营 - 'Dajing Camp' – likely the name of a military encampment or outpost. No direct historical reference is known, so it should be translated as is.
  • 参戎 - A military rank or title; possibly 'assistant general' or 'military officer.'
  • 马成龙 - A proper name; the person is likely a military officer or commander.
  • 塔 - A name; the subject of the narrative, possibly a military officer or traveler.
  • 麻皮 - A term that may refer to a specific skin condition or scar, possibly from a natural or supernatural event. In classical Chinese, it can also be used metaphorically.
  • 年貌册 - A record or register of age and physical appearance, likely used in official or military records.
  • 狞飚 - A rare term for a violent wind or storm, possibly a supernatural or mythological phenomenon.
  • 宦海 - A metaphor for the world of officialdom, often used to describe the political career of a scholar-official, with its ups and downs.

Proper Nouns

  • 塔 - A name; likely a military officer or traveler. Should be translated as 'Tǎ' or 'Tat.'
  • 马成龙 - A name; likely a military officer. Should be translated as 'Ma Chenglong.'
  • 大靖营 - A proper name for a military camp or location. Should be translated as 'Dajing Camp' or 'Camp Dajing.'
  • 参戎 - A title or rank; likely a military position. Should be translated as 'Assistant General' or 'Military Officer.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大眼睛 - Literal descriptive phrase meaning 'big eyes' or 'large eyeball,' used to describe a physical anomaly.
  • 夜坐读书 - Common phrase indicating scholarly activity, meaning 'sitting at night reading books.'
  • 忽见一物，类蝙蝠 - Describes an unexpected sight, meaning 'suddenly saw something, resembling a bat.'
  • 急以手格之 - Indicates a sudden reaction, meaning 'quickly used his hand to hit it.'
  • 拍然坠地 - Describes the sound and action of the object falling, meaning 'it fell to the ground with a thud.'
  • 化一大眼睛，阔数寸 - Describes the transformation, meaning 'it transformed into a large eyeball, several inches wide.'
  • 绕地旋转不息，久之方灭 - Describes the movement and eventual disappearance of the eyeball, meaning 'it rotated around the ground without stopping, eventually disappearing after a long time.'
  • 积聚数十年 - Indicates a long period of saving, meaning 'accumulated for many years.'
  • 蓄白金十两 - Refers to a significant amount of wealth, meaning 'stored ten taels of white gold.'
  • 熔成锭，盛以荷囊 - Describes the method of storage, meaning 'melted it into ingots and stored them in a lotus bag.'
  • 什袭胸前 - Indicates a high level of care and secrecy, meaning 'wrapped it in ten layers and kept it close to the chest.'
  • 一日，忽失之 - Indicates a sudden loss, meaning 'one day, it suddenly disappeared.'
  • 遍觅不可复得 - Describes the unsuccessful search, meaning 'searched everywhere but could not find it again.'
  • 冥想亦不记忆失于何所 - Indicates the monk's confusion and distress, meaning 'even in deep thought, he could not remember where it was lost.'
  • 日久成疾，颓然不起 - Describes the physical and mental toll of the loss, meaning 'after a long time, he fell ill and could not get up.'
  • 举寺悉知其以失金所致 - Indicates the community's understanding of the cause, meaning 'the entire temple knew that his illness was caused by losing the gold.'
  • 罔不悼惜 - Expresses the collective sympathy, meaning 'no one did not mourn and pity him.'
  • 寺中溷坑深且阔 - Describes the physical setting, meaning 'the temple's latrine pit was deep and wide.'
  • 积秽满 - Indicates the state of the latrine, meaning 'filled with accumulated filth.'
  • 例雇人净之 - Describes the regular maintenance, meaning 'it was customary to hire someone to clean it.'
  • 适净人出粪，得一虾蟆 - Describes the unexpected discovery, meaning 'just as the cleaner was removing the waste, he found a toad.'
  • 大如升 - Describes the size of the toad, meaning 'as big as a winnowing basket.'
  • 紧抱一物，不少放松 - Describes the toad's grip, meaning 'tightly holding an object, not letting go at all.'
  • 力劈之 - Indicates the effort to free the object, meaning 'forced to break it open.'
  • 见一荷囊，内贮金一锭，约十两 - Describes the contents of the bag, meaning 'found a lotus bag containing one ingot of gold, about ten taels.'
  • 众僧莫知所自 - Indicates the mystery surrounding the gold, meaning 'the monks did not know where it came from.'
  • 且时际隆冬，虾蟆何以独生 - Questions the unusual circumstances, meaning 'and it was during the severe winter, how could a toad survive alone.'
  • 猜疑间，忽忆某僧失金事 - Indicates the connection made, meaning 'while guessing and suspecting, suddenly remembered the monk who lost the gold.'
  • 持以示之，僧乃蹶然而兴 - Describes the monk's reaction, meaning 'showed it to him, and the monk suddenly stood up.'
  • 虾蟆倏不见 - Indicates the toad's vanishing, meaning 'the toad suddenly disappeared.'
  • 识者谓是僧精神之所凝结而化成也 - Provides a supernatural explanation, meaning 'those who understood said it was the monk's spirit condensed and transformed into the toad.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 一日，报有虎，奇踊跃而往，果有虎，大倍如常，黄质白章，从者惊以为异，谏止，不听，弃马掉锏而前 - Describes a specific encounter with a tiger. Can be read as: 'One day, someone reported a tiger. Xue eagerly went to it. Indeed, there was a tiger, much larger than usual, with a yellow body and white patterns. His followers were surprised and thought it strange, advising him to stop, but he did not listen. He abandoned his horse and swung his mace forward.'

  • 史巫纷若 - Vivid description of the chaos or busyness of the shrine, with '史巫' referring to historians and shamans.
  • 越不测何事 - Means 'I wonder what mysterious or unexplainable event is happening,' expressing curiosity or confusion.
  • 觅庙主将询之 - Means 'I sought out the temple master to ask him,' indicating an attempt to find someone for information.
  • 堂中坐立多人 - Means 'there were many people sitting and standing in the hall,' describing a crowded space.
  • 庙主已死，僵卧床上，臭且 - Means 'the temple master had died, lying stiff on the bed, with a foul smell,' describing a grim scene.
  • 越惊而走出 - Means 'I was startled and walked out,' expressing a sudden reaction to the scene.
  • 忽一巫见而抱持之 - Means 'suddenly a shaman saw me and held me,' describing an unexpected encounter.
  • 弃鼓投地 - Means 'threw down his drum,' indicating a dramatic gesture.
  • 崩角稽首 - Means 'bowed with his head touching the ground,' a sign of deep reverence or submission.
  • 大言曰：“公子自至耶？” - Means 'he shouted loudly, 'Has the young master come here by himself?' expressing surprise or recognition.
  • 呼众至 - Means 'called the crowd over,' indicating a call to attention.
  • 告曰：“此即新任神圣之长公子也，寻亲至此，纯孝动天。” - Means 'he announced, 'This is the eldest son of the newly appointed divine being, who has come here in search of his family, and his pure filial piety has moved heaven.'' This is a dramatic and exaggerated statement, likely meant to elevate the status of the protagonist.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 土地祠 - A small shrine dedicated to a local deity or spirit, often found in rural areas, reflecting traditional Chinese religious practices.
  • 史巫 - Historians and shamans, indicating the blending of historical and spiritual roles in traditional Chinese society.
  • 神前牲醴错陈 - Sacrificial offerings and wine before a deity, reflecting traditional Chinese rituals and beliefs.
  • 崩角稽首 - A deep bow with the head touching the ground, a sign of utmost respect or submission, reflecting traditional Chinese etiquette.
  • 纯孝动天 - The idea of pure filial piety moving heaven, reflecting the Confucian value of filial piety and its spiritual significance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening phrase '徘徊逾时，心伤如割' sets the emotional tone but is elliptical, requiring context to fully understand the protagonist's state of mind.
  • The long sequence '信步行数里路，见一土地祠，拟入祠谋一餐，入则人聚如蚁，神前牲醴错陈，史巫纷若' describes the protagonist's journey and the scene at the shrine, requiring careful parsing to maintain the flow and meaning.
  • The sequence '越不测何事，觅庙主将询之，入其室，堂中坐立多人，庙主已死，僵卧床上，臭且' describes the protagonist's curiosity, the temple master's death, and the grim scene, requiring careful handling to maintain the narrative flow.
  • The sequence '越惊而走出，忽一巫见而抱持之，弃鼓投地，崩角稽首，大言曰：“公子自至耶？”' describes the protagonist's reaction and the shaman's dramatic gesture, requiring careful handling to maintain the dramatic tension.
  • The sequence '呼众至，告曰：“此即新任神圣之长公子也，寻亲至此，纯孝动天。”' describes the shaman's announcement and the crowd's reaction, requiring careful handling to maintain the dramatic and exaggerated tone.

  • 古刹 - Literal meaning: 'ancient temple.' A common term for an old Buddhist temple.
  • 危阁 - Literal meaning: 'perilous pavilion.' Suggests a high and precarious structure.
  • 题额 - Literal meaning: 'inscribed name.' Refers to the plaque or sign above a building.

  • 恍然悟 - Literal meaning: 'suddenly realized.' Indicates a sudden moment of understanding.
  • 沐浴具衣冠 - Literal meaning: 'took a bath and put on ceremonial clothes.' A ritual act of purification and preparation.
  • 屏去僮仆 - Literal meaning: 'dismissed the servants.' Indicates a desire for solitude.
  • 端坐楼上而逝 - Literal meaning: 'sat upright on the upper floor and passed away.' Describes a dignified and composed death.
  • 音乐声 - Literal meaning: 'music sound.' Refers to a heavenly or spiritual music.
  • 逾时始歇 - Literal meaning: 'after a while it stopped.' Indicates the duration of the music.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 棘闱 - Refers to the examination hall or the site of the imperial examination, often used metaphorically to denote the place where scholars take the civil service exam.
  • 果报之异 - Means 'strange phenomena of retribution,' referring to unusual occurrences that are believed to be the result of karma or divine justice.
  • 见于棘闱者尤著 - Means 'those seen in the examination hall are especially prominent,' highlighting the significance of such phenomena in the context of the imperial exams.
  • 执事官公服致诚以召鬼神 - Means 'the officials in charge, dressed in official robes, sincerely summon ghosts and spirits,' describing the ritual performed by officials before the exam begins.
  • 有冤者报冤，有仇者报仇 - Means 'those with grievances will seek retribution, those with grudges will seek revenge,' a call to the spirits to address any wrongs or conflicts.
  • 举子入场之前一夕 - Means 'the night before the candidates enter the examination hall,' indicating the timing of the ritual.
  • 皓月今宵满，红颜往日残 - A poetic line meaning 'the bright moon is full tonight, but the beauty of the past is now faded,' expressing a sense of nostalgia and loss.
  • 召讫，插三色旗于明远楼四角 - Means 'after summoning, they insert three-colored flags at the corners of the Mingyuan Tower,' describing the placement of the flags after the ritual.
  • 吏且招且呼曰 - Means 'the officials call out while summoning,' indicating the dual action of calling and summoning during the ritual.
  • 故场中怪异叠见，愈出愈奇 - Means 'therefore, strange and unusual phenomena appear repeatedly, becoming increasingly bizarre,' describing the frequent occurrence of strange events during the exam.
  • 予以招神招鬼之事质之 - Means 'I questioned them about the matter of summoning spirits,' indicating the author's inquiry into the ritual.
  • 因举所闻之尤异者记八则 - Means 'therefore, I recorded eight particularly strange cases that I have heard,' indicating the purpose of the text.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 棘闱 - The examination hall, a crucial site in the imperial examination system, where scholars would take the civil service exams.
  • 果报之异 - The concept of karma and divine retribution, deeply rooted in Chinese philosophy and religion, particularly Buddhism and Taoism.
  • 执事官 - Officials responsible for conducting the imperial exams, often high-ranking officials or scholars.
  • 明远楼 - A tower in the examination hall, often used for announcements and rituals.

  • 招神招鬼 - Rituals to summon spirits, common in traditional Chinese culture, often performed to ensure fairness and justice.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 結伴入南闱 - To go to the southern examination hall in a group, referring to taking the imperial exams.
  • 甫毕头场 - Just finished the first session, referring to the initial part of the exam.
  • 治任 - To pack one's belongings, often used to indicate preparation for departure.
  • 言语支吾 - To speak evasively or hesitantly, indicating reluctance to disclose information.
  • 颜色凄楚 - To have a sad or distressed appearance, suggesting emotional distress.
  • 不得已始明告 - To finally admit reluctantly, indicating that the person was forced to reveal something they did not want to.
  • 言之丑矣 - It is a shameful thing to say, indicating that the topic is embarrassing or difficult to discuss.
  • 先君子 - Refers to one's father or a respected elder.
  • 宦游半世 - To have spent half a lifetime traveling for official duties, indicating a long career in government service.
  • 解组归 - To retire from office, indicating the end of a career.
  • 病怔忡 - To suffer from palpitations or anxiety, indicating a mental or emotional illness.
  • 数年不愈 - To remain untreated for several years, indicating a long-term illness.
  • 捐馆 - To pass away, a euphemism for death.
  • 榻前 - At the bedside, indicating a place where someone is close to death.
  • 昧心事 - To have done something that goes against one's conscience, indicating a morally questionable act.
  • 任某县令时 - When serving as the magistrate of a certain county, indicating a specific period in the person's career.
  • 受贿二千金 - To accept a bribe of two thousand taels of silver, indicating a corrupt act.
  • 冤杀二囚 - To wrongfully kill two prisoners, indicating a grave injustice.
  • 大罪恶 - Great sins, indicating serious moral transgressions.
  • 阴报当斩嗣 - To be punished by the underworld with the execution of one's descendants, indicating severe karmic retribution.
  • 祖上有拯溺功 - The ancestors had the merit of rescuing someone from drowning, indicating a positive deed by the ancestors.
  • 五世不得温饱 - Five generations will not enjoy comfort and abundance, indicating long-term suffering.
  • 人非高于泰山 - One's status is not higher than Mount Tai, indicating that no one is above the consequences of their actions.
  • 鬼责深于沧海 - The responsibility to the spirits is deeper than the sea, indicating severe spiritual accountability.
  • 地狱之设 - The existence of hell, indicating a place of punishment.
  • 幸脱无由 - There is no way to escape, indicating that there is no escape from the consequences.
  • 子孙或不知命 - Descendants might not know their fate, indicating that future generations might not understand the consequences.
  • 妄想功名 - To foolishly pursue fame and success, indicating misguided ambitions.

  • 乾隆 - Refers to the Qianlong Emperor, the sixth emperor of the Qing dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 后兄弟相继死，唯我仅存 - A narrative opening meaning 'After my brothers died one after another, only I remained.'
  • 乡试二次，悉被墨渖污卷 - Refers to two failed attempts at the provincial examination, where the scrolls were stained with ink, indicating failure.
  • 昨在棘中，文思颇涌 - 'In the examination hall yesterday, my thoughts flowed freely,' using '棘中' (examination hall) as a metaphor for the rigorous testing environment.
  • 三更即脱稿 - 'By the third watch, I had finished my essay,' where '脱稿' means completing a written work.
  • 倏一人披帷而入 - 'Suddenly, a person entered through the curtain,' using '倏' (suddenly) to emphasize the unexpected nature of the event.
  • 惊神之，乃先君也 - 'I was shocked and saw it was my late father,' where '惊神之' (shocked and startled) conveys the emotional impact.
  • 颜色愁苦，怒责予曰 - 'His face was sad and angry, and he scolded me, saying,' describing the emotional state and action of the late father.
  • 奈何忘我遗嘱，屡为非分 - 'Why do you forget my last words and repeatedly act beyond your station?' where '遗嘱' (last words) and '非分' (beyond one's station) are key phrases.
  • 致我奔走道途，辛苦备尝 - 'Causing me to run around and suffer greatly,' where '辛苦备尝' (suffer greatly) emphasizes the extent of the hardship.
  • 若再不悛，祸不旋踵矣 - 'If you do not repent, disaster will follow immediately,' where '祸不旋踵' (disaster will follow immediately) is a vivid idiom.
  • 随以手械一击，烛灭砚翻 - 'He struck me with a hand tool, extinguishing the candle and overturning the inkstone,' where '手械' (hand tool) and '烛灭砚翻' (candle extinguished, inkstone overturned) describe the physical impact.
  • 旋失所在 - 'He then disappeared,' where '旋失所在' (disappeared) conveys the sudden vanishing.
  • 予惊走而恸 - 'I fled in shock and wept bitterly,' where '惊走而恸' (fled in shock and wept bitterly) captures the emotional response.
  • 比栉胥来致询 - 'Neighbors and officials came to inquire,' where '比栉' (neighbors) and '胥来致询' (came to inquire) describe the social reaction.
  • 见予油墨满卷，各嗟叹而散 - 'Seeing my scroll covered in ink, they all sighed and left,' where '油墨满卷' (covered in ink) and '各嗟叹而散' (sighed and left) convey the scene.
  • 予今年二十有五，登蓝榜 - 'I am now twenty-five and have passed the blue list,' where '蓝榜' (blue list) refers to the successful candidates in the imperial examination.
  • 不足为恨，所痛先人负谴 - 'It is not worth regretting, but what pains me is that my ancestors are punished,' where '不足为恨' (not worth regretting) and '先人负谴' (ancestors are punished) express the narrator's feelings.
  • 拘击九幽 - 'Imprisoned in the underworld,' where '拘击九幽' (imprisoned in the underworld) is a metaphor for severe punishment.
  • 行当削发入山，披缁出世 - 'I will soon shave my head and enter the mountains, becoming a monk and leaving the world,' where '削发入山' (shave head and enter the mountains) and '披缁出世' (become a monk and leave the world) describe the narrator's decision.
  • 学目连大士，救拔亡灵 - 'I will learn from the Bodhisattva Maudgalyayana, rescuing the spirits of the dead,' where '学目连大士' (learn from the Bodhisattva Maudgalyayana) and '救拔亡灵' (rescue the spirits of the dead) are key phrases.
  • 忏悔之情，幸诸君垂鉴焉 - 'My feelings of repentance, I hope you all take note of,' where '忏悔之情' (feelings of repentance) and '垂鉴' (take note of) express the narrator's plea.
  • 众闻之，靡不咋舌神惊 - 'Everyone heard this and was amazed and shocked,' where '靡不咋舌神惊' (amazed and shocked) describes the reaction.
  • 善念为之一炽 - 'Their good intentions were greatly stirred,' where '善念为之一炽' (good intentions were greatly stirred) conveys the emotional impact.
  • 先生退而作《归山诗》以送之 - 'The master then composed a poem titled 'Returning to the Mountains' to send him off,' where '退而作《归山诗》' (composed a poem titled 'Returning to the Mountains') and '以送之' (to send him off) describe the action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乡试 - The provincial examination, one of the stages of the imperial examination system.

  • 蓝榜 - The list of successful candidates in the imperial examination, often referred to as the 'blue list.'
  • 目连大士 - The Bodhisattva Maudgalyayana, known for his ability to rescue the spirits of the dead.
  • 披缁 - To become a monk, derived from the color of the robes worn by Buddhist monks.
  • 九幽 - A metaphor for the underworld or hell.
  • 削发 - To shave one's head, a common practice for monks and nuns.

  • 目连大士 - The Bodhisattva Maudgalyayana.
  • 九幽 - The underworld or hell.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 获罪于天，无所祷也 - Proverbial phrase meaning 'If one offends Heaven, there is no prayer that can help.' Emphasizes the severity of offending divine justice.
  • 升堂鼓响，阶下者如对阎罗 - Vivid simile comparing the sound of drums in a court to the presence of Yama (阎罗), the king of hell, suggesting the gravity and finality of judgment.
  • 覆盆冤成，受刑者恍遭地狱 - Simile comparing the plight of the wrongfully accused to being in hell, emphasizing the injustice and suffering.
  • 嗷嗷赤子 - Term for innocent children, often used to evoke sympathy.
  • 延颈于父母之堂 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'stretching out their necks to their parents' house,' implying a sense of hope and trust.
  • 簇簇黄金，已私受于夤缘之吏 - Phrase describing bribes or favors being accepted by corrupt officials.
  • 沉冤莫雪于生前 - Phrase meaning 'unresolved grievances that cannot be cleared before death.'
  • 宿孽旋生于死后 - Phrase meaning 'past sins that quickly manifest after death.'
  • 三战三黜，子孙五世贫寒 - Phrase describing repeated failures and the resulting poverty passed down through generations.
  • 一代一丁，兄弟崇朝殂谢 - Phrase describing the rapid decline and death of family members within a short period.
  • 可不慎欤？可不戒欤？ - Rhetorical questions emphasizing the need for caution and vigilance.
  • 某科会试，一江南举人，入头场，文战至二更，往末号解手，三更不返 - Detailed account of an incident during the imperial examinations.
  • 见一物大如牛，白如雪，倚墙根蠕动，霍霍有声 - Vivid description of a supernatural or monstrous entity.
  • 心殊恐怖，大声叱之，物忽起立，乃是一白人，面作青白色，两眼大如鸡子，碧而有光 - Detailed description of the entity's appearance and the protagonist's reaction.
  • 身如梦魇，呼叫不能出声，亦不自知僵仆之由也 - Phrase describing a state of paralysis and unconsciousness.
  • 曳白而出 - Phrase meaning 'to leave without completing the examination,' indicating failure.

  • 覆盆冤成 - Reference to the metaphor of a bowl covering a pot, symbolizing the difficulty of clearing a wrongfully accused person's name.
  • 赤子 - Term for innocent children, often used in moral and ethical contexts.
  • 夤缘之吏 - Corrupt officials who use their connections to accept bribes or favors.
  • 沉冤莫雪 - Common theme in Chinese literature and folklore, emphasizing the injustice of unresolved grievances.
  • 宿孽 - Past sins or karmic debts that manifest in the present or future.
  • 崇朝 - Short period of time, often used to describe the rapid decline or death of someone.
  • 会试 - Imperial examination held every three years, crucial for selecting government officials.
  • 江南 - Region in southern China, historically known for its cultural and economic significance.

Proper Nouns

  • 兰岩 - Name of the author or compiler.
  • 阎罗 - King of hell.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 因出一纸示康，其辞曰 - Formal expression meaning 'he then took out a piece of paper to show Kang; the words he said were as follows.'
  • 夜迢修而转侧兮，心似焚以怦怦 - Poetic expression describing a restless night, literally 'the night is long and winding, and I toss and turn; my heart feels like it is burning and beating.'
  • 缘幽兰之早折兮，悼芳蕙之先零 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'because the fragrant orchid was broken early; lamenting the early withering of the fragrant orchid,' symbolizing the premature loss of something precious.
  • 何恶莸之枝蔓兮，甚贼苗之稂莠 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why does the evil weed spread its branches; the thief's seed is like a weed,' symbolizing the spread of corruption.
  • 欲剪拔以粪除兮，足而棘刺手 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'I want to cut it out and remove it with fertilizer; my feet are stung by thorns, and my hands are pricked,' symbolizing the desire to eliminate corruption and the difficulties and obstacles in the process.
  • 告田父以假其锄锸兮，络冒头而钳制口 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'I ask the farmer to lend me his spade and mattock; my head is covered, and my mouth is clamped,' symbolizing the need for help from others and the constraints and silencing of truth.
  • 冀美人于一晤兮，倏神结而为梦 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'I hope to meet the beautiful lady once; suddenly, the spirit ties itself into a dream,' symbolizing a longing for a meaningful encounter and the fleeting nature of hopes and dreams.
  • 出衱阇以暇瞩兮，见蓬颗之蔽冢 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'I step out of the temple to look around leisurely; I see the grave covered by weeds,' symbolizing a moment of reflection and the neglect and decay of something once valued.
  • 声嘤嘤以启悲兮，先秋风而听之 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the sound of crying opens up sorrow; I hear it before the autumn wind,' symbolizing the emotional impact of loss and the anticipation of sorrow.
  • 魂冉冉其欲离乎窀穸兮，犹逡巡以鼠思 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'my soul slowly wants to leave the tomb; still hesitating like a mouse,' symbolizing the desire for release from suffering and the indecision and fear.
  • 羌儃佪而夷犹兮，非畴昔之娇态 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'indeed, hesitating and lingering; not the delicate demeanor of the past,' symbolizing the indecision and lingering and the change in appearance or state.
  • 频拭目以端睨兮，徒神奔而鬼怪 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'I frequently wipe my eyes and look straight ahead; only spirits run and ghosts appear,' symbolizing a moment of clarity or realization and the chaos and confusion of the situation.
  • 讵绮罗之化蝶兮，体袒裼而裸裎 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'how could the silk and brocade transform into butterflies; the body is exposed and naked,' symbolizing the transformation of something beautiful and vulnerability and exposure.
  • 袁冰玉之销铄兮，怆以纵横 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the ice and jade of Yuan are melted; sorrow spreads in all directions,' symbolizing the loss of purity and integrity and the pervasive nature of grief.
  • 妾薄命以贻戚兮，职王孙之故也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'I have a thin fate and bring sorrow; it is due to the fault of Wangsun,' symbolizing a sense of personal misfortune and blame or responsibility.
  • 君独生以曷欢兮，宁不怀兹楚也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'you alone live, what joy do you have; how can you not feel this sorrow,' symbolizing a sense of loneliness and lack of fulfillment and the inevitability of sorrow.
  • 吾将与子同穴兮，心则石而力则锦 - Formal expression meaning 'I will be with you in the same grave; my heart is as hard as stone, and my strength is as soft as silk,' symbolizing a paradoxical state of being.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 神氣為鬼所奪，所以不壽耳 - Classical causal statement: 'Because their vital energy has been taken by ghosts, that is why they do not live long.'
  • 次日黎明 - Common classical expression for 'the next day at dawn.'
  • 執祛審視 - 'To grasp the sleeve and examine closely,' a classical way of describing someone inspecting something by hand.
  • 初大如升，漸如碗，如杯 - Metaphorical phrase using *升* (unit of volume), *碗* (bowl), and *杯* (cup) to indicate growth in size.
  • 竊異之 - 'Secretly found it strange,' with *竊* as a modest particle meaning 'secretly' or 'privately.'
  • 結舌不能對 - 'Unable to speak or answer,' conveying that the person was speechless.
  • 得毋回煞之說，未可盡诬乎 - Classical rhetorical question: 'Could it not be that the belief in returning souls is not entirely false?'
  • 幸我月令尚好 - Metaphorical use of *月令* (monthly orders) to mean 'my monthly condition is still good.'
  • 其不得殃也幾希矣 - 'The chance that I would not be affected is very small.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 回煞 - Traditional Chinese belief in the return of the soul or ghost after death, occurring on the third day after death.
  • 塾師 - Private tutor or teacher in a private school or family household.
  • 館僮 - Servant or attendant in a household, specifically in the study or living quarters of a scholar or official.
  • 炕頭 - Platform or raised bed used for sleeping or sitting in traditional Chinese homes, especially in northern China.
  • 炕洞 - Heating passage or flue beneath the *炕*, used in traditional Chinese homes for heating.
  • 月令 - Normally refers to the traditional Chinese *Yue Ling* (Monthly Orders), but in this context, it is used metaphorically to refer to the person’s health or condition during a particular time.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼 - Refers to supernatural beings, often associated with the dead or spirits.

  • 不亦冤乎 - Expresses sympathy; literally 'isn't that a pity?'
  • 子在咸安寓时 - Indicates the time and place of the story; literally 'when I was staying in Xianan.'
  • 闻同学隆君兴言 - Introduces the storyteller; literally 'I heard my classmate Long Junxing say.'
  • 有小儿夜啼 - Describes the problem; literally 'there was a child who cried at night.'
  • 越两月不愈 - Indicates the duration of the problem; literally 'it lasted for two months without healing.'
  • 有老妪识为夜星子 - Describes the supernatural element; literally 'there was an old woman who identified it as a night star child.'
  • 自云能捉之 - Introduces the old woman's claim; literally 'she said she could catch it.'
  • 问所需，无难办者 - Indicates the ease of providing what was needed; literally 'when asked what she needed, there was nothing difficult to provide.'
  • 唯用木作方笼 - Describes the construction of the cage; literally 'only use wood to make a square cage.'
  • 四面糊白纸 - Describes the decoration of the cage; literally 'cover the four sides with white paper.'
  • 罨灶上 - Indicates the placement of the cage; literally 'cover the stove.'
  • 灶窟内设油灯一盏 - Describes the setup; literally 'place an oil lamp inside the stove cavity.'
  • 燃之，光射纸上 - Describes the effect of the lamp; literally 'light it, and the light shines on the paper.'
  • 俟小儿啼作 - Indicates the timing of the action; literally 'wait until the child starts crying.'
  • 即灶前覆一粗磁碗 - Describes the setup; literally 'then cover a coarse porcelain bowl in front of the stove.'
  • 碗上横置一菜刀 - Describes the placement of the knife; literally 'place a kitchen knife horizontally on the bowl.'
  • 踞小凳面灶门而坐 - Describes the position of the old woman; literally 'sit on a small stool facing the stove door.'
  • 家人悉令回避 - Indicates the precautions taken; literally 'all family members were ordered to stay away.'
  • 童男稚女则弗禁 - Indicates the exception; literally 'but young boys and girls were not restricted.'
  • 食顷 - A unit of time, roughly equivalent to the time it takes to eat a meal.
  • 灯骤暗 - Describes a sudden change; literally 'the lamp suddenly went dark.'
  • 纸上隐隐见黑影 - Describes the appearance of the shadow; literally 'a dark shadow appeared faintly on the paper.'
  • 往来闪烁不定 - Describes the movement of the shadow; literally 'moving and flickering uncertainly.'
  • 或人、或马、或猫犬 - Describes the various forms of the shadow; literally 'sometimes human, sometimes horse, sometimes cat or dog.'
  • 悉仿佛其形 - Indicates the similarity to real forms; literally 'all resembling their shapes.'
  • 妪诅咒愈急 - Describes the old woman's reaction; literally 'the old woman cursed more urgently.'
  • 黑影往来愈伙 - Describes the increased activity of the shadow; literally 'the dark shadow moved more frequently.'
  • 最后一影 - Indicates the final form; literally 'the last shadow.'
  • 色黯黝 - Describes the color of the shadow; literally 'dark and black.'
  • 映纸独真 - Indicates the clarity of the shadow; literally 'clearly reflected on the paper.'
  • 止而不动 - Describes the final state of the shadow; literally 'stopped and did not move.'
  • 形颇似槥 - Describes the final form; literally 'the shape was quite like a coffin.'
  • 妪急举刀背 - Describes the old woman's action; literally 'the old woman quickly raised the back of the knife.'
  • 力碎覆碗 - Describes the breaking of the bowl; literally 'with force broke the covered bowl.'
  • 砉然一声 - Describes the sound of the breaking; literally 'a sharp sound.'
  • 烛中灯忽大明 - Describes the sudden brightness; literally 'the lamp in the candle suddenly became bright.'
  • 黑影印纸上不灭 - Describes the lasting effect; literally 'the dark shadow remained on the paper without fading.'
  • 妪举笼以火焚之 - Describes the final action; literally 'the old woman raised the cage and burned it with fire.'
  • 儿啼顿止 - Describes the resolution of the problem; literally 'the child's crying stopped immediately.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 如此怪异 - Colloquial expression meaning 'so strange' or 'so unusual,' used to describe the bizarre nature of the events.
  • 遍叩亲故，皆无有 - Elliptical sentence meaning 'They searched all relatives and friends, but found none.'
  • 遂成讼，有司严刑拷掠，不能成狱 - Sequence of events: 'They filed a lawsuit, and the authorities subjected them to harsh interrogation, but could not establish a case.'
  • 村后固接乱山，人踪罕到，唯刍荛雉兔者，间一过焉 - Describes the setting: 'Behind the village lies a rugged mountain range, rarely visited by people, only occasionally passed by those who cut grass, gather firewood, hunt pheasants, and catch rabbits.'
  • 一樵入林伐木，于万树中，见一人悬柏树上，目大如盏，举两手作扑人状，声吱吱若鸣蝙蝠，身摇摇如戏秋千 - Detailed scene: 'A woodcutter entered the forest to cut wood and, among the thousands of trees, saw a person hanging from a pine tree, eyes as large as bowls, raising both hands as if to strike someone, making a squeaking sound like a bat, and body swaying back and forth like a swing.'
  • 狂奔下山，述于村人 - Sequence of actions: 'He ran madly down the mountain and told the villagers.'
  • 村人聚众制梃，鼓勇而往，四面击之，良久不动 - Collective action: 'The villagers gathered and armed themselves with sticks, rallied their courage, and went to attack the body from all sides, striking it for a long time without it moving.'
  • 李之邻里咸在，其服色虽旧犹，犹辨为胡氏女 - Description: 'Li's neighbors were all present, and although her clothing was old, they could still recognize her as the Hu family woman.'
  • 白其父母令观之，真实不虚 - Sequence of actions: 'They informed her parents to come and see, and it was indeed true.'
  • 抚尸大恸，因验而焚之，臭达数里，叽叽作声，疑案始结 - Final actions: 'They embraced the corpse and wept bitterly, then examined and burned it, the stench reaching several miles, making a chirping sound, and the mysterious case was finally resolved.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 有司 - Refers to government officials or authorities, indicating the involvement of officialdom in the case.

  • 刍荛雉兔 - Refers to people who cut grass, gather firewood, hunt pheasants, and catch rabbits, indicating rural occupations.
  • 秋千 - Refers to a swing, a common playground equipment in Chinese culture.

  • The phrase '不如舍之' is a comparative expression meaning 'it would be better' or 'it is not as good,' which introduces a recommendation.
  • The phrase '负而投诸河' is a compound action where '负' (carried) and '投诸河' (threw into the river) are linked by '而' (and), indicating a sequence of actions.
  • The phrase '甫出城' is a temporal marker indicating an immediate action after leaving the city.
  • The phrase '囊骤穴' is somewhat ambiguous but can be interpreted as 'the sack suddenly made a hole,' where '骤' (suddenly) modifies the action of '穴' (making a hole).
  • The phrase '临河而返' is a compound action where '临河' (reached the river) and '返' (returned) are linked by '而' (and), indicating a sequence of actions.
  • The phrase '猫已先归' is a temporal marker indicating that the cat had already returned before the other events.
  • The phrase '直至寝室' is a temporal marker indicating that the events continued until they reached the bedroom.
  • The phrase '启帘而入' is a compound action where '启帘' (opened the curtain) and '入' (entered) are linked by '而' (and), indicating a sequence of actions.
  • The phrase '公子兄弟方咸集父母侧论猫事' is a temporal marker indicating that the young master and his brothers were just gathering.
  • The phrase '瞥见猫来' is a compound action where '瞥见' (saw) and '猫来' (the cat coming) are linked, indicating a sequence of actions.
  • The phrase '胥发怔' is a state of being where '胥' (all) and '发怔' (were stunned) are linked, indicating a collective reaction.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 猫登踞胡床 - Literal description of the cat sitting on a trundle bed, potentially carrying metaphorical or symbolic meaning.
  • 怒视其父，目眥欲裂 - Vivid description of intense anger, literally meaning 'glared at its father, eyes nearly popping out.'
  • 张须切齿 - Describes a threatening posture, literally meaning 'furrowed its whiskers and bared its teeth.'
  • 厉声而骂曰 - Sets the tone for the following speech, literally meaning 'shouted angrily and said.'
  • 何物老奴！尸诸余气，乃欲谋溺杀我耶？ - Harsh accusation, literally meaning 'What kind of old slave! With the remnants of life, you dare to plot to drown me?'
  • 在汝家，自当推汝为翁；若在我家，云乃辈犹可耳孙，汝奈何丧心至此？ - Rhetorical question emphasizing the speaker's superiority, literally meaning 'In your home, you should naturally be the master; but in my home, you are just a great-grandson, how could you lose your mind to this extent?'
  • 且汝家祸在萧墙，不旋踵而至，不自惊怕，而谋杀我，岂非大谬！ - Critique of the father's actions, literally meaning 'Moreover, your family's disaster is within the palace walls, it will come soon, you should be afraid, but you plot to kill me, is that not a great mistake!'
  • 汝盍亦自省平日之所为乎？ - Call for self-reflection, literally meaning 'Why don't you also reflect on your daily actions?'
  • 生具螾蚁之材，夤缘得禄。 - Sarcastic remark, literally meaning 'Born with the talents of a worm or ant, you gained your position through connections.'
  • 初仕刑部，以距得上官心。 - Describes the father's career, literally meaning 'Initially serving in the Ministry of Punishment, you gained the favor of your superiors.'
  • 出知二州，愈事贪酷。 - Criticizes the father's governance, literally meaning 'When you were governor of two states, you became even more greedy and cruel.'
  • 桁杨斧锧，威福自诩。 - Describes the father's arrogance, literally meaning 'With shackles and axes, you boasted of your power and favor.'
  • 作官二十年，草菅人命者，不知凡几。 - Serious accusation, literally meaning 'Having served as an official for twenty years, you have killed countless lives.'
  • 尚思恬退林泉，正命牖下，妄想极矣！ - Sarcastic remark, literally meaning 'Still thinking of retiring to the mountains and forests, living a peaceful life, what a wild dream!'
  • 所谓兽心人面，汝实人中妖孽，乃反以我言为怪，真怪事也！ - Strong condemnation, literally meaning 'So-called beastly heart and human face, you are truly a monster among people, yet you find my words strange, what a strange thing!'
  • 遂大骂不已，辱及所生。 - Describes the intensity of the cat's anger, literally meaning 'Continued to curse endlessly, even insulting the mother.'
  • 举室纷拏，莫不抢攘。 - Describes the scene of confusion, literally meaning 'The entire household was in chaos, everyone was in a panic.'
  • 或挥古剑，或掷铜瓶，茗碗香炉，尽作攻击之具。 - Describes the violent actions, literally meaning 'Some swung ancient swords, others threw copper bottles, tea bowls and incense burners were all used as weapons.'
  • 猫哂笑而起曰：“我去，我去，汝不久败坏之家，我不谋与汝辈争也。” - The cat's final statement, literally meaning 'The cat laughed and stood up, saying, 'I'm leaving, I'm leaving, your soon-to-be-ruined family, I don't intend to compete with you.'
  • 亟出户，缘树而逝，至此不复再至。 - Describes the cat's departure, literally meaning 'Quickly left the house, climbed the tree and disappeared, never to return again.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 恶少 - Refers to a ruffian or bully, a common figure in classical Chinese literature.
  • 侯 - Refers to the young man, likely a noble or respected individual.
  • 骑者 - Refers to riders, possibly local officials or travelers, who intervene in the situation.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 犬在旁大嗥来啮 - The phrase '大嗥来啮' can be read as 'howled loudly and came to bite' or 'howled and came to bite,' both conveying the same meaning but with slightly different emphasis.
  • 乃尽褫侯衣，不留寸缕，缚手足，俯而捺诸草中，欲淫之 - This long sequence of actions must be read as a single, continuous event, describing the ruffian's cruel and degrading treatment of the young man.
  • 侯哭叫声嘶，滚地不定 - The phrase '滚地不定' can be read as 'rolling around on the ground uncontrollably' or 'rolling around on the ground without stopping,' both conveying the same sense of distress.
  • 是夜梦犬作人言，谓侯曰 - The dream sequence is a clear break from the narrative, introducing a supernatural element that must be clearly marked in the translation.
  • 觉以为异，谨志之 - The final clause '觉以为异，谨志之' can be read as 'Upon waking, he found it strange and carefully recorded it,' emphasizing the young man's reaction to the dream.

  • 面如土色 - A vivid description of someone turning pale, indicating fear or shock.
  • 复其旧 - Refers to returning to normal, indicating a recovery from shock or fear.
  • 述之 - Refers to narrating or recounting, indicating the act of telling a story.

Idiomatic Expressions
