Idiomatic Expressions

  • 比至园，日已夕矣 - Temporal phrase meaning 'By the time he arrived at the garden, the day had already turned to evening.'
  • 荒榛绕径，丰草堆阶 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'overgrown with thorny bushes around the path, and thick grass piled up on the steps.'
  • 门亦扃锁 - Phrase meaning 'the gate was also locked.'
  • 搴衣拨莽，越垝垣以入 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'lifting his clothes and pushing through the overgrowth, he climbed over the broken wall to enter.'
  • 纡回循径行 - Phrase meaning 'winding his way along the path.'
  • 忽达一桥 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly reaching a bridge.'
  • 朱栏摧折，红板朽残 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'the red railings were broken and the red planks were rotten and decayed.'
  • 桥下芦荻丛生，蛙鸣积漈 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'under the bridge, reeds and rushes grew thickly, and frogs croaked in the accumulated water.'
  • 过桥抵一轩 - Phrase meaning 'crossing the bridge, he reached a pavilion.'
  • 蛛丝当户，纱绿在窗 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'cobwebs hung in the doorway, and green silk hung in the windows.'
  • 生徙倚栏干，徘徊忘返 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'the young man leaned against the railings, wandered back and forth, and forgot to return.'
  • 不觉古墙月上，苔砌中喧 - Phrase meaning 'unaware that the moon had risen behind the ancient wall, the moss-covered steps were noisy.'
  • 晚风入树林间，如闻吟啸 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the evening wind entered the forest, and it seemed as if someone was singing and whistling.'
  • 本欲穷其幽邃 - Phrase meaning 'he originally intended to explore its deep and secluded areas.'
  • 当此际心殊怛怖 - Phrase meaning 'at this moment, his heart was particularly filled with fear.'
  • 乃折轩前凤仙花，却步欲归 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'he then picked a fuchsia flower in front of the pavilion, took a step back, and wanted to return.'
  • 忽闻回廊下，有清锐其音者 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly, he heard a clear and sharp voice from under the corridor.'
  • 叱曰 - Phrase meaning 'shouted, saying.'
  • 何处小蛮奴，擅入人家窥伺 - Phrase meaning 'Where is this little barbarian,擅自闯入别人家窥探?'
  • 贵人眷属居此，肯容汝折一茎草，踏一块砖耶 - Phrase meaning 'The noble people's family lives here, would they allow you to pick a single blade of grass or step on a single brick?'
  • 生惊视之 - Phrase meaning 'The young man was startled and looked at them.'
  • 则十六七二女嬛也 - Phrase meaning 'Then he saw two young women, about sixteen or seventeen years old.'
  • 一绿衣，一碧衣，眉目如画，面无怒色，但作恶声耳 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'One wore a green robe, the other a blue robe, their eyebrows and eyes were like paintings, their faces showed no anger, but they made a harsh sound.'
  • 生自知冒昧 - Phrase meaning 'The young man knew he had acted rashly.'
  • 急弃花整衣 - Phrase meaning 'He quickly threw away the flower and straightened his clothes.'
  • 趋而揖之曰 - Phrase meaning 'He hurried forward and bowed, saying.'
  • 异乡年少，孤客无知，孟浪采花，罪不容赦 - Phrase meaning 'A young stranger from a foreign land, a lonely guest who acted without knowledge, rashly picked a flower, a crime that cannot be forgiven.'
  • 倘蒙宽宥，佩德不忘 - Phrase meaning 'If you could grant me leniency, I will never forget your kindness.'
  • 或即宽宥，亦平常事，那便是德，那便不忘 - Phrase meaning 'Even if you were to grant leniency, it would be a common thing, that would be kindness, that would be unforgettable.'
  • 书痴便绐，欲绐阿谁耶 - Phrase meaning 'A bookworm would deceive, who would he deceive?'
  • 今不痛加惩治，彼以为我辈孱弱，必源源而来矣 - Phrase meaning 'If we do not severely punish him now, he will think we are weak and will come again and again.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 轩 - Refers to a pavilion or a small building, often used in classical literature to describe a place of beauty or seclusion.
  • 凤仙花 - Refers to the fuchsia flower, a common ornamental plant in Chinese gardens.
  • 回廊 - Refers to a covered walkway or corridor, often found in traditional Chinese architecture.
  • 贵人眷属 - Refers to the family or relatives of a noble person, indicating a high social status.
  • 书痴 - Refers to someone who is overly fond of books or literature, often used to describe a scholar or a studious person.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This phrase describes the dilapidated state of the bridge, adding to the atmosphere of neglect and decay.
  • This phrase creates a vivid image of the natural environment under the bridge, with reeds and frogs.
  • This phrase describes the pavilion, with cobwebs and green silk, suggesting a long-abandoned and eerie place.
  • This phrase describes the young man's actions, indicating his fascination and hesitation.
  • This phrase indicates the passage of time and the changing environment, with the moon rising and the steps becoming noisy.
  • This phrase describes the natural sounds, creating a sense of mystery and solitude.
  • This phrase explains the young man's original intention and his sudden fear.
  • This phrase describes his reaction to the fear, picking a flower and wanting to leave.
  • This phrase introduces the unexpected encounter with the two young women.
  • This phrase is a harsh accusation, indicating the young women's anger and suspicion.
  • This phrase emphasizes the high status of the residents and the severity of the young man's actions.
  • This phrase describes the appearance of the two young women, with a focus on their beauty and age.
  • This phrase provides a detailed description of the young women, highlighting their appearance and demeanor.
  • This phrase describes the young man's reaction to the accusation, showing his realization of his mistake and his attempt to apologize.
  • This phrase is the young man's explanation, emphasizing his youth and lack of knowledge.
  • This phrase is a plea for leniency, expressing gratitude and remembrance.
  • This phrase is a sarcastic response, indicating that leniency would be a common thing and not a significant act of kindness.
  • This phrase is a criticism of the young man, suggesting that he is trying to deceive.
  • This phrase explains the young women's reasoning for punishing the young man, indicating that leniency would be seen as weakness and encourage further trespassing.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 言次 - Formal expression meaning 'while speaking' or 'in the course of conversation.'
  • 復有數女奴 - Means 'there were several female servants' or 'several female slaves appeared again.'
  • 自轩后出 - Means 'came out from behind the screen' or 'emerged from behind the curtain.'
  • 何事喋喋 - Rhetorical question meaning 'what are you talking about so much?' or 'why are you chattering?'
  • 娘子候回话矣 - Means 'Madam is waiting for a reply' or 'Madam is expecting an answer.'
  • 回底话 - Colloquial expression meaning 'the reply' or 'the answer.'
  • 知他何处书生 - Means 'who knows where this scholar is from' or 'how would we know where this bookish person is from?'

  • 令人一字不解 - Means 'it's incomprehensible to me' or 'I can't understand a word of it.'
  • 捉去听娘子发付之 - Means 'take him away to hear what Madam has to say' or 'catch him and take him to hear what Madam says.'

  • 大惧 - Means 'great fear' or 'extreme terror.'
  • 投地求释 - Means 'threw himself on the ground to beg for release' or 'fell to the ground pleading for mercy.'
  • 置若罔闻 - Means 'ignored' or 'paid no attention.'

  • 拥发辫 - Means 'grabbed his hair' or 'tugged his braids.'
  • 后推而前挽之 - Means 'pushed him from behind and pulled him from the front' or 'pushed him back and pulled him forward.'
  • 固无缚鸡力 - Means 'he had no strength to tie up a chicken' or 'he was too weak to catch a chicken.'
  • 遭此纷拏 - Means 'faced this chaos' or 'endured this turmoil.'

  • 广厅下 - Means 'in the large hall' or 'under the spacious hall.'

  • 散去 - Means 'dispersed' or 'dispersed themselves.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 女奴 - Refers to female servants or slaves, common in historical settings.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the appearance of several female servants and their question.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 二女挽生入左室 - Two women led the guest into the left room.

  • 昨夜梦神人见告，故能预知郎名姓里居，幸郎勿猜也 - Last night, I dreamed of a divine person who told me, so I could foresee your name, surname, and hometown. Please do not guess.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鄞江 - Refers to Yinzhou, a region in present-day Ningbo, Zhejiang Province.

  • 陇西 - Refers to Longxi, a region in present-day Gansu Province.
  • 秦州 - Refers to Qin Prefecture, a historical administrative region in present-day Gansu Province.
  • 参戎 - Refers to a military commander, a high-ranking military official.
  • 红丝系足 - A metaphor for destiny or fate, often used in Chinese folklore and literature to describe predestined relationships.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谨志不忘 - to remember carefully.
  • 于郎固有德 - the lady has indeed done me a favor.
  • 何可便忘 - how could I forget so soon?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鸡犬 - refers to the legend of the immortal Han Fei, where his followers were transformed into immortals.
  • 上大罗天 - refers to the highest level of heaven in Daoist cosmology.
  • 碭 - a character that is difficult to translate directly, but it is often used to describe a bird's cry, which is why it is mentioned as sounding like a bird.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 随闻内城蒲牢声，如海鲸之鸣 - Vivid simile comparing the sound inside the inner city to the roar of a whale in the sea, emphasizing its loudness and power.
  • 知漏下矣 - Classical expression meaning 'knowing that the water clock has fallen,' indicating the passage of time or the onset of night.
  • 无非珍异 - Means 'nothing but rare and precious,' highlighting the exceptional nature of the food.
  • 尤多不知名者 - Means 'especially many that are unknown,' suggesting the presence of unfamiliar or exotic items.
  • 与郎毕姻后，仍返故宅耳 - Formal expression meaning 'after marrying the young man, I will return to my original home,' indicating a temporary arrangement.
  • 卿先世作何官 - Polite way of asking about someone's ancestors' official positions.
  • 底事呴呴呕呕，如老妇然 - Humorous or teasing remark meaning 'why are you fussing and arguing like an old woman?'

  • 生颊为之赤 - Means 'the young man's cheeks turned red,' indicating embarrassment.
  • 举觞自罚 - Means 'to take a cup and punish oneself,' a gesture of self-reprimand.

  • 红烛高烧，金炉香袅 - Describes a romantic setting with '红烛' (red candles) burning brightly and '金炉' (golden incense burner) emitting fragrant smoke.
  • 恍游天上，如在梦中矣 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'as if floating in heaven, like being in a dream,' describing a state of bliss or enchantment.

Proper Nouns

  • 娟 - The name of the female character, likely a courtesan or noblewoman.
  • 生 - The name of the male character, likely a young man or scholar.

  • 漏 - Refers to an ancient water clock, a device used to measure time.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 娟有异术 - Juan has strange skills or possesses extraordinary abilities, referring to supernatural or magical powers.
  • 收取各种花子 - Collect various seeds of flowers; '花子' may be symbolic or magical in context.
  • 祝之，化为异香 - Pray over them, and they transform into strange fragrances; a magical transformation, likely alchemy or enchantment.
  • 含之，齿舌俱馥 - Inhale it, and both teeth and tongue are filled with fragrance; a poetic expression of intense aroma.
  • 又能摄取诸物，从心所欲 - She can also summon various objects, according to the heart's desire; a magical ability to teleport or summon objects at will.
  • 顷刻至前 - In an instant, they arrive before you; a common expression in Classical Chinese to denote speed.
  • 助荔枝杨梅之难至者 - Help those who find it difficult to obtain lychees and longans; refers to magical assistance in acquiring rare fruits.
  • 莫不应之如响 - None fail to respond as if to an echo; a metaphor for immediate and perfect response.
  • 可检点作归计矣 - You should now prepare for your return; a polite but firm suggestion to begin the journey home.
  • 以我车来，以尔贿迁 - Bring my chariot, and with your gifts I shall move; a direct translation of a classical verse from the *Book of Songs*, expressing a request for assistance or support in moving.
  • 无需于君，但劳玉趾一行耳 - No need for you, just trouble your noble feet to come once; a polite way of saying the speaker can handle things but would appreciate the listener's presence.
  • 日间或不便，夜去可也 - It may be inconvenient during the day, but you can leave at night; suggests a preference for a more private or magical departure.
  • 男妇来者甚众，见娟与生，皆下拜 - Many men and women came, and upon seeing Juan and the man, all knelt in reverence; indicates high status or magical powers.
  • 几榻箱笼，争相负荷，须臾而尽 - Chairs, beds, boxes, and trunks were all loaded by them, and in a moment they were all gone; describes rapid and efficient movement of goods.
  • 惟自忖度，非夙有仙缘乌能得此 - Only by reflecting on it, one realizes that without a predestined connection to immortality, one could not attain this; a philosophical or mystical reflection on fate and spiritual destiny.
  • 虽南面百城，弗与易矣 - Even if one ruled a hundred cities facing south (i.e., a king), it would not be exchanged for this; a metaphor for the value of spiritual or magical existence over worldly power.
  • 既而入室，陈设尤华美 - Afterward, entering the room, the furnishings were especially luxurious; a description of the interior space.
  • 食餍甘肥，衣厌细软 - Feasting on sweet and rich foods, clothing in the softest fabrics; expressions of indulgence and comfort.
  • 息功名之念，绝乡国之思 - Abandoning thoughts of fame and achievement, cutting off thoughts of one's homeland; a philosophical or spiritual detachment from worldly concerns.
  • 转盼已逾两月 - In the blink of an eye, two months had passed; a metaphor for the rapid passage of time, often used in Classical Chinese to express the subjective experience of time.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 花子 - A term that could be interpreted literally as 'flower seeds,' but in this context may be symbolic or magical, as it is used in a transformation (化为异香).
  • 祝之 - 'To pray over' or 'to invoke,' a term common in Daoist or magical contexts.
  • 摄取诸物 - 'To summon or teleport objects' — a magical ability.
  • 从心所欲 - 'According to the heart's desire' — a phrase from the *Analects* of Confucius, suggesting complete freedom or control.
  • 荔枝杨梅 - 'Lychee and longan' — both are tropical fruits native to southern China, often associated with luxury or delicacy.
  • 以我车来，以尔贿迁 - A direct quote from the *Book of Songs* (《诗经》), specifically from the poem *Shengmin* (《生民》), used here as a poetic or metaphorical expression of assistance or movement.
  • 玉趾 - 'Jade feet' — a respectful and poetic way to refer to someone's feet, often used in classical poetry.
  • 南面百城 - 'Facing south over a hundred cities' — a metaphor for ruling a kingdom or being a king, as the ruler of a state would sit facing south in ancient China.
  • 仙缘 - 'Immortal connection' — a term from Daoist or mystical traditions, referring to a predestined relationship with immortality or spiritual realms.
  • 转盼 - 'In the blink of an eye' — a classical expression for the rapid passage of time, often used in mystical or poetic contexts.

  • 计当日窥见至隐，止吴姁一人 - '计' means to calculate, '当日' means 'that day,' '窥见' means 'to see secretly,' '至隐' means 'the most hidden things,' and '止' means 'only.' This phrase means 'On that day, only Wu Xu saw the most hidden things.'
  • 苟秘而不宣，焉能泄漏 - '苟' means 'if,' '秘而不宣' means 'kept secret and not announced,' and '焉能泄漏' means 'how could it leak out?' This phrase means 'If it was kept secret and not announced, how could it leak out?'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生貼符卻回，涕泣求救 - A compact description of the young man's actions and emotional state: returning with a charm and weeping for help.
  • 莘為畫策曰 - Introduces the advice given by Xun.
  • 娟雖淫毒，然通靈有術 - Describes the character of Juan, highlighting her dual nature: licentious and poisonous, yet spiritually adept and magical.
  • 避之不密，彼終能蹤跡之 - Explains the consequences of not avoiding her carefully: she will eventually track them down.
  • 非求黃道士符錄不可 - Emphasizes the necessity of seeking the talisman from the Yellow Daoist.
  • 黃道士援太乙秘笈，持五雷正法 - Describes the Yellow Daoist's practices: consulting the secret scriptures of the Supreme One and practicing the Five Thunder orthodox methods.
  • 住五岳觀，郎屈節求之，符即可得 - Outlines the steps to obtain the talisman: residing in the Five Peaks Temple and humbling oneself to the young man.
  • 此事不得緩，三日內不得，則大事去矣 - Stresses the urgency of the matter: if not obtained within three days, the major problem will be lost.
  • 彼每出不返者，以仍有人被惑故也 - Explains why she does not return: because there are still people being deceived by her.
  • 此際必幻化園墅，相與流連 - Describes her current activities: transforming into a garden or villa and lingering together.
  • 如郎朝死，夕即有他人入室 - Warns of the consequences of not acting: if the young man dies in the morning, someone else will enter the room in the evening.
  • 蓋此間為狐之巢穴 - Reveals the true nature of the place: a den for foxes, symbolizing deceit and danger.
  • 雖出千里，遲數年，終須歸此耳 - Suggests the inevitability of returning: even if one goes a thousand miles away, one must eventually return here after several years.
  • 彼出已兩日矣，再三五日當返 - Provides a timeline for her return: she has been out for two days already and will return in another three to five days.
  • 郎欲逃，正其時也 - Indicates the right time to escape: now is the right time for the young man to escape.
  • 且悲且喜，再拜謝曰 - Describes the emotional state and action: both sad and happy, bowing twice and thanking.
  • 卿起白骨而肉之，何以圖報 - Expresses gratitude: you revived my body from bones, how can I repay you?
  • 寧生離，無死別，行矣，慎之勿相忘！緣盡於此矣 - Expresses a desire to remain connected: I would rather be separated in life than die apart, go, be careful and don't forget, our缘分 ends here.
  • 累卿將奈何 - Expresses concern: what should I do about burdening you?
  • 兒聞賢者急病而讓夷 - Refers to a moral principle: I have heard that the wise prioritize urgent matters and yield to righteousness.
  • 況兒亦有術，自能發付老魅，無慮也 - Expresses confidence: besides, I also have skills, I can handle the old demon myself, there is no need to worry.
  • 於是再四促之，生不得已，握手辭行，哭失聲，莘急止之 - Describes the final farewell: thus, he urged him repeatedly, the young man had no choice but to shake hands and bid farewell, he cried out loud, and Xun quickly stopped him.

  • 黄熟视而咍曰 - Means 'the Yellow Daoist looked at him carefully and laughed.' '黄' (huáng) refers to the Yellow Daoist, '熟视' (shú shì) means 'looked at carefully,' '而' (ér) is a conjunction, '咍' (hāi) means 'to laugh,' and '曰' (yuē) means 'said.'
  • 妖气濡染未深，何伤弓惊饵之早？ - Means 'The evil influence has not deeply penetrated, why are you so alarmed so early?' '妖气' (yāo qì) means 'evil influence,' '濡染' (rú rǎn) means 'to be influenced or stained,' '未深' (wèi shēn) means 'not deeply,' '何' (hé) is an interrogative particle, '伤弓惊饵' (shāng gōng jīng ěr) is an idiom meaning 'to be overly cautious or alarmed,' and '之早' (zhī zǎo) means 'so early.'
  • 子真机警人哉！ - Means 'You are truly a cautious person!' '子' (zǐ) is a respectful term for 'you,' '真' (zhēn) means 'truly,' '机警' (jī jǐng) means 'cautious' or 'alert,' and '人哉' (rén zāi) is an exclamatory phrase meaning 'you are a person indeed.'
  • 书三符付之曰 - Means 'wrote three talismans and gave them to him, saying.' '书' (shū) means 'to write,' '三符' (sān fú) means 'three talismans,' '付之' (fù zhī) means 'gave them to him,' and '曰' (yuē) means 'said.'
  • 终生佩之可也 - Means 'You can wear them all your life.' '终生' (zhōng shēng) means 'all your life,' '佩之' (pèi zhī) means 'wear them,' and '可也' (kě yě) means 'can do so.'
  • 径来所寓寺中 - Means 'went directly to the temple where he was staying.' '径来' (jìng lái) means 'went directly,' '所寓' (suǒ yù) means 'where he was staying,' and '寺中' (sì zhōng) means 'in the temple.'
  • 寺僧见而惊曰 - Means 'The temple monk saw him and was surprised, saying.' '寺僧' (sì sēng) means 'temple monk,' '见而' (jiàn ér) means 'saw and,' '惊曰' (jīng yuē) means 'was surprised and said.'
  • 先生一晌在何处？ - Means 'Where have you been for a moment?' '先生' (xiān shēng) is a respectful term for 'sir' or 'mister,' '一晌' (yī shǎng) means 'a moment,' and '在何处' (zài hé chǔ) means 'where.'
  • 致老僧怀惑至今 - Means 'Leaving the old monk confused until now.' '致' (zhì) means 'caused,' '老僧' (lǎo sēng) means 'old monk,' '怀惑' (huái huò) means 'confused,' and '至今' (zhì jīn) means 'until now.'
  • 生以诡词绐之 - Means 'The man used deceptive words to deceive him.' '生' (shēng) refers to the main character, '以' (yǐ) means 'with,' '诡词' (guǐ cí) means 'deceptive words,' and '绐之' (dài zhī) means 'to deceive him.'
  • 问僮仆焉往 - Means 'asked where the servants had gone.' '问' (wèn) means 'asked,' '僮仆' (tóng pú) means 'servants,' and '焉往' (yān wǎng) means 'where had they gone.'
  • 僧曰 - Means 'The monk said.' '僧' (sēng) means 'monk,' and '曰' (yuē) means 'said.'
  • 回南月余矣 - Means 'They have been back in the south for more than a month.' '回南' (huí nán) means 'back in the south,' '月余' (yuè yú) means 'more than a month,' and '矣' (yǐ) is a particle indicating completed action.
  • 行李尚存乎？ - Means 'Is the luggage still there?' '行李' (háng lǐ) means 'luggage,' '尚存' (shàng cún) means 'still there,' and '乎' (hū) is an interrogative particle.
  • 曰 - Means 'said.' '曰' (yuē) is a verb meaning 'to say.'
  • 携去矣 - Means 'They took it away.' '携去' (xié qù) means 'took it away,' and '矣' (yǐ) is a particle indicating completed action.
  • 惝怳无措手足处 - Means 'Feeling lost and not knowing what to do.' '惝怳' (chǎng huǎng) means 'feeling lost,' and '无措手足处' (wú cuò shǒu zú chù) means 'not knowing what to do.'
  • 僧曰 - Means 'The monk said.' '僧' (sēng) means 'monk,' and '曰' (yuē) means 'said.'
  • 先生岂无亲故仕于京师者，盍往就之？ - Means 'Do you not have relatives or acquaintances who serve in the capital? Why don't you go to them?' '先生' (xiān shēng) is a respectful term for 'sir' or 'mister,' '岂无' (qǐ wú) means 'do not have,' '亲故' (qīn gù) means 'relatives or acquaintances,' '仕于京师' (shì yú jīng shī) means 'who serve in the capital,' '盍往就之' (hé wǎng jiù zhī) means 'why don't you go to them?'
  • 生曰 - Means 'The man said.' '生' (shēng) refers to the main character, and '曰' (yuē) means 'said.'
  • 有亲属为部郎，往岁已左迁外补矣。今也则无。 - Means 'I have relatives who are officials, but they were demoted last year and transferred to a remote post. Now I have none.' '有' (yǒu) means 'have,' '亲属' (qīn shǔ) means 'relatives,' '为部郎' (wéi bù láng) means 'who are officials,' '往岁' (wǎng suì) means 'last year,' '已左迁外补' (yǐ zuǒ qiān wài bǔ) means 'were demoted and transferred to a remote post,' and '今也则无' (jīn yě zé wú) means 'now I have none.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 纵朋友怜而顾我 - Concessive phrase meaning 'even if friends pity and look after me,' indicating a hypothetical scenario.
  • 我何面目见之 - Rhetorical question meaning 'what face can I show them,' expressing shame or embarrassment.
  • 况任黎交谊，世有几人 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how many in the world have such a deep friendship,' emphasizing the rarity of such bonds.
  • 倘觌面云泥 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'if we meet, it would be like meeting someone from the sky and someone from the earth,' indicating a vast difference in status or ability.
  • 情何以堪 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how can I bear it emotionally,' expressing emotional distress.
  • 先生固才貌兼者 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'you are indeed someone who combines talent and appearance,' praising the person's qualities.
  • 怀策以谒时贤，投刺以干当道 - Formal expression meaning 'carry your plans to visit contemporary scholars and present your credentials to influential people,' describing the act of seeking patronage.
  • 必得上贡天子，何衣食之足虑乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'you will surely be able to present your talents to the emperor, so why worry about food and clothing,' expressing confidence in the person's future.
  • 落拓如此 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'in such a state of poverty and despair,' indicating a dire situation.
  • 谁复肯斡旋者 - Rhetorical question meaning 'who would be willing to help,' expressing a lack of support.
  • 自媒不遂 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'if self-promotion fails,' indicating a failed attempt to gain recognition.
  • 身辱名裂 - Compound phrase meaning 'lose one's reputation and dignity,' expressing the consequences of failure.
  • 宁冻馁以填沟壑，不忍摇尾向人也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'rather than starve to death in a ditch, I would not beg for help,' expressing a strong sense of pride and independence.
  • 往者余弗及，来者余弗闻 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the past I cannot reach, and the future I cannot hear,' expressing the limitations of human perception.
  • 老僧眼中所见之士，先生一人而已 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the only person I have seen in my eyes,' indicating the uniqueness of the person.
  • 糊口有地，而深耻托钵 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'having a place to eat, yet deeply ashamed to beg,' expressing a sense of pride.
  • 爱其品节，重其羔雁 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'admire his moral integrity and value his offerings,' expressing respect and admiration.
  • 先生尚志，非长贫贱者 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'you have lofty aspirations and are not destined for poverty and lowly status,' expressing high hopes for the person.
  • 请设一榻，而屈先生卖赋长安，以待时至，不亦可乎 - Formal expression meaning 'please set up a bed for you, and let you sell your essays in Chang'an, waiting for the right opportunity, is this not possible,' offering a practical solution.
  • 生乃感谢，便栖寺中，为人代书 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'he then thanked him and stayed in the temple, writing for others,' describing the person's actions.

  • 天子 - Refers to the emperor, the supreme ruler.

  • 摇尾向人 - A metaphor for begging or fawning.
  • 老僧 - Refers to an old monk or a wise elder.
  • 长安 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital city during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 卖赋 - Refers to selling literary works or essays.
  • 代书 - Refers to writing on behalf of others.

  • 即索观玉章 - Means 'immediately asked to see the jade seal.'
  • 生取诸怀 - Means 'the young man took it from his bosom.'

  • 审辨色 - Means 'examined the color carefully.'
  • 询曰 - Means 'asked, saying.'

  • 坐石小歇 - Means 'sat on a stone to rest briefly.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 亦謝僧百金 - Also thanked the monk with a hundred gold coins; '亦' emphasizes the action of thanking, and '百金' refers to a significant sum of money.
  • 始以情告之 - Then told him the truth; '始' indicates the beginning of a new phase in the narrative, and '以情告之' means to reveal one's true feelings or situation.
  • 荼荠不同亩 - Weeds and chrysanthemums should not be planted in the same field; metaphorical expression suggesting that different things should not be mixed, especially in moral or spiritual matters.
  • 早图之 - Act early on it; '早' means 'early,' and '图之' means 'to plan or act on it,' emphasizing the urgency of the matter.
  • 沐以香汤 - Washed with fragrant water; '沐' means 'to wash,' and '香汤' refers to scented water, likely used for purification or respect.
  • 裹以锦襦 - Wrapped in brocade robes; '裹' means 'to wrap,' and '锦襦' refers to luxurious robes, symbolizing respect and care.
  • 纳诸槥中 - Placed them in the coffins; '纳' means 'to place,' '诸' is a pronoun meaning 'them,' and '槥' refers to a coffin.
  • 祭而复归 - After the ceremony, he returned; '祭' means 'to perform a ceremony,' and '复归' means 'to return.'
  • 永诀矣 - Farewell forever; '永诀' is a formal way of saying goodbye, implying that the meeting will never happen again.
  • 纸灰飞起，旋转绕墓三匝 - The paper ashes flew up, rotating around the grave three times; '飞起' means 'flew up,' '旋转' means 'rotated,' and '绕墓三匝' means 'circled the grave three times,' symbolizing a ritual or sign of respect.
  • 知有神气也 - It was known that there was a divine presence; '知' means 'to know,' '有' means 'there is,' and '神气' refers to a divine or supernatural presence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 螟蛉 - The term originates from a mythological belief that ants would adopt caterpillars as their own offspring, symbolizing adoption.
  • 骐骥 - A metaphor for talented individuals, often used to describe capable siblings or students.

  • 详述 - Providing a detailed account or explanation, often used in formal settings to convey important information.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 药石之言 - Refers to wise and corrective advice, often used metaphorically to describe advice that is both beneficial and somewhat harsh.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a sequence of actions: Lu requests to leave, two young men try to stop him by holding back his reins and cutting his stirrup, but Lu cannot be stopped.
  • This sentence introduces Hu, the son of a prominent family, who pays respects and proposes marriage.
  • This sentence is a detailed refusal of the marriage proposal, explaining Lu's circumstances and declining the offer.
  • This sentence is a detailed argument in favor of accepting the marriage proposal, emphasizing the potential benefits and the risks of remaining alone.
  • This sentence describes Lu's decision to accept the proposal, the gifts he offers, and his gratitude.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening rhetorical comparison argues from the greater case to the lesser one: '自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎.'
  • The clause '此事遍於經史' is elliptical but means that examples of this principle are found throughout the classics and histories.
  • The closing sequence '吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳' compresses concession, limitation, and purpose.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然則遂無腐時乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Then is there never a time when it decays?' or 'Then is there no time of decay?' Implies a challenge or skepticism.
  • 玉晶何腐之有 - Response to the previous question, literally 'How could jade crystal decay?' A metaphorical statement suggesting that something pure or uncorrupted cannot decay.
  • 見卵而求時夜 - Seeing an egg and seeking a rooster’s crowing at dawn. A metaphor for seeking something (the rooster’s crowing) from a source that cannot produce it (an egg).
  • 痴絕妄絕矣 - Absurdly foolish and wildly deluded. A compound expression emphasizing extreme foolishness and delusion.
  • 何異荒祠木居士 - How different is this from a desolate shrine’s wooden idol? A metaphor for something lifeless or without real power.
  • 不慮風雨，而梁柱蠹生 - Not considering wind and rain, yet the beams and pillars are infested with worms. A metaphor for neglecting external dangers, leading to internal decay.
  • 庇蔭我者，即所以摧毀我者也 - That which shelters me is also that which destroys me. A paradoxical statement indicating that the very thing that protects can also cause destruction.
  • 豈必外来之風雨哉 - Why must it be the external wind and rain? A rhetorical question emphasizing that the destruction comes from within or from the neglect of the structure itself.
  • 斧斤不施，樗散之所以壽也 - If axes are not used, the chu san (a type of tree) will live long. A metaphor for avoiding destruction by not being cut down, implying that inaction can be a form of preservation.
  • 文采章身，鳳凰之所以隱也 - If one displays brilliance and marks on the body, the phoenix hides. A metaphor for how displaying one’s talents or virtues may lead to being hidden or avoided by others.
  • 子謀隸仙籍，而先窺鬼箓 - You are seeking to be listed among the immortal records, yet you first spy on the records of ghosts. A metaphor for seeking divine status but engaging in forbidden or worldly matters first.
  • 吾恐子平之願難畢，而賈誼之易來 - I fear your wish for peace (zi ping) will be difficult to complete, while the misfortunes of Jia Yi will easily come. A reference to the historical figure Jia Yi, known for his misfortunes and early death, used as a warning.
  • 鐵鑄一個錯不成 - A single iron mistake cannot be cast. A metaphor for the inevitability of failure or the permanence of a mistake.
  • 恓惶甚矣 - Very distressed and anxious. An emotional expression of worry or sorrow.
  • 即如館中戴監生 - Take for example the Dai Jiansheng in the hall. This is a reference to a specific person, likely a scholar or official.
  • 本非科甲中人 - Originally not someone from the imperial examination system. A reference to someone who did not pass the civil service exams.
  • 尚瞆瞆憂思，自加戕賊 - Still in a daze of worry, harming oneself. A metaphor for self-destructive behavior due to anxiety.
  • 齊彭殤 - To be equal in death and life. Refers to the idea of equating life and death, or being indifferent to longevity or mortality.
  • 未可與老夫較修短也 - Not yet can you compare with an old man in terms of length of life. A metaphor for comparing life spans or longevity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 玉晶 - Jade crystal, a metaphor for something pure and incorruptible. It may also be a metaphor for a person of high moral character.
  • 時夜 - The crowing of the rooster at dawn, a symbol of order and time.
  • 荒祠木居士 - A wooden idol in a desolate shrine, a metaphor for something lifeless or without true power.
  • 梁柱蠹生 - Worms infesting the beams and pillars, a metaphor for internal decay or corruption.
  • 庇蔭我者，即所以摧毀我者也 - This line reflects a Daoist or philosophical concept of paradox and the duality of protection and destruction.
  • 斧斤不施，樗散之所以壽也 - Chu San is a type of tree known for its slow growth and longevity. This line is a metaphor for inaction leading to preservation.
  • 文采章身 - Brilliance and markings on the body, a metaphor for showing off one’s virtues or talents.
  • 鳳凰之所以隱也 - The phoenix hides, a metaphor for how displaying one’s virtues may lead to being hidden or overlooked.
  • 子謀隸仙籍 - You are seeking to be listed among the immortal records, a reference to the idea of becoming an immortal or being enshrined in heaven.
  • 鬼箓 - Ghost records, a reference to the underworld or forbidden knowledge.
  • 子平 - A reference to the concept of 'zi ping' (子平), which is a system of Chinese astrology and fortune-telling, often associated with longevity and destiny.
  • 賈誼 - Jia Yi (c. 200–168 BCE), a famous Han dynasty scholar and statesman, known for his early death and misfortunes.
  • 戴監生 - Dai Jiansheng, likely a reference to a scholar or official who had passed the imperial examination, but the term 'jiansheng' (監生) refers to someone who was granted a scholar’s status without passing the exams.
  • 科甲 - Refers to the imperial examination system, the 'ka' (科) being the subject of study and the 'jia' (甲) being the ranking.
  • 齊彭殤 - A reference to the ancient Chinese idea of equating life and death, or being indifferent to longevity, derived from the story of Peng and Shang, who were said to live for hundreds of years.
  • 修短 - Length and shortness, a metaphor for life span or longevity.

  • 试不第 - Refers to failing the imperial examinations, a significant event in the lives of scholars in imperial China.
  • 投笔经商 - A metaphorical expression meaning to abandon scholarly pursuits and engage in business, often used to describe a shift in career or lifestyle.

  • 永落孫山 - Reference to the imperial examination system, meaning 'permanently failing to pass the exams' or 'always ending up at the bottom of the list,' as 'Mount Sun' (孫山) was the name of the last person on the list of successful examinees.

  • 正陽門 - One of the main gates of the Beijing city wall during the Ming and Qing dynasties. It was a significant location in the capital.
  • 夜二更后 - Refers to the time of night in the traditional Chinese system of timekeeping, where the night is divided into five 'gengs' (更). The second geng corresponds to approximately 9 PM to 11 PM.
  • 家人環守通宵，抢攘不休 - Describes the family staying up all night, agitated and restless, likely due to concern for the afflicted person.

  • 正陽門 - Proper noun referring to the Zhengyang Gate, a major gate in the Beijing city wall during the Ming and Qing dynasties.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 邻人或言 - Colloquial expression meaning 'a neighbor said' or 'someone from the neighborhood mentioned.'
  • 有巫而走无常者 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'a witch who serves as a messenger of the netherworld.' '走无常' refers to the role of a messenger or guide for spirits.
  • 最能祛除不祥 - Means 'most skilled at dispelling misfortune or evil.' '祛除' means to remove or eliminate, and '不祥' refers to misfortune or evil.
  • 盍使治之 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why not have him treat it?' '盍' is an archaic particle used to suggest a course of action.
  • 家人亦夙耳其名 - Means 'the family had heard of his name before.' '夙耳' is an archaic expression meaning 'had heard of' or 'was familiar with.'
  • 亟往祈请 - Means 'hurriedly went to pray and request.' '亟' means urgently or quickly, and '祈请' means to pray or request.
  • 佟未至，傅已知之 - Means 'Tong had not arrived, but Fu already knew about it.' '傅' likely refers to the person who was supposed to be treated.
  • 哂且骂曰 - Means 'smiled and scolded, saying.' '哂' means to smile with contempt, and '骂' means to scold or curse.
  • 无论铜觭角，铁觭角，又何为哉 - Rhetorical statement meaning 'whether it's copper Jijiao or iron Jijiao, what difference does it make?' '无论' means 'regardless of,' and '又何为哉' means 'what difference does it make?'
  • 男女观者如堵墙 - Means 'men and women watching were like a wall.' '如堵墙' is a metaphor for a large crowd.
  • 瞠目视之曰 - Means 'stared at it, saying.' '瞠目' means to stare in amazement or anger.
  • 何处鬼魅，敢来此处祟人 - Means 'where are the ghosts or evil spirits that dare to come here to harm people?' '祟人' means to harm or torment people.
  • 不实供，即叉汝下油锅矣 - Means 'if you don't confess honestly, I'll throw you into the oil pot.' '不实供' means 'not confess honestly,' and '叉汝下油锅' means 'throw you into the oil pot with a fork.'
  • 吱吱切齿不已 - Means 'gritted his teeth continuously, making a sound.' '吱吱' imitates the sound of grating teeth, and '切齿' means to grit one's teeth.
  • 命倾油于巨镬中 - Means 'ordered the oil to be poured into a large pot.' '倾油' means to pour oil, and '巨镬' means a large cooking pot.
  • 烧柴煎之 - Means 'burned wood to heat it.' '烧柴' means to burn wood, and '煎之' means to heat or cook.
  • 油沸 - Means 'the oil boiled.'
  • 旋捉一钢叉 - Means 'quickly grabbed a steel fork.' '旋' means quickly or immediately.
  • 向傅面上旋绕 - Means 'swung the fork around Fu's face.' '旋绕' means to swing or move in a circular motion.
  • 故振响其环以恐吓之 - Means 'intentionally shaking the ring on the fork to scare him.' '振响' means to shake and make a sound, and '恐吓' means to scare or intimidate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 巫而走无常者 - Refers to a witch or sorcerer who serves as a messenger for spirits or the netherworld. This is a common figure in Chinese folklore and religious practices.
  • 号佟觭角 - '佟觭角' (Tong Jijiao) is a name that may have a specific cultural or regional significance, but it is not widely known. The name '觭角' (jijiao) could refer to a horn or a peculiar feature, possibly symbolizing a unique or supernatural ability.
  • 祛除不祥 - The practice of dispelling misfortune or evil is deeply rooted in Chinese folk beliefs and religious rituals, often involving exorcisms, prayers, and other forms of spiritual cleansing.
  • 男女观者如堵墙 - The metaphor of a crowd being like a wall suggests a large, dense gathering of people, which is a common way to describe a crowd in classical Chinese literature.
  • 油锅 - The oil pot is a symbol of punishment in Chinese folklore, often used in exorcisms or as a threat to force someone to confess or reveal secrets.

Proper Nouns

  • 佟觭角 - The name 'Tong Jijiao' is a proper noun that should be consistently translated as such.
  • 傅 - The name 'Fu' is a proper noun that should be consistently translated as such.
  • 某胡同 - 'A certain alley' or 'a specific lane' is a proper noun that should be consistently translated as such.
  • 油锅 - 'Oil pot' is a cultural term that should be consistently translated as such.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 傅哆口长号曰 - Fang opened his mouth and cried out, a description of Fang's emotional outburst.
  • 嗟乎，冤哉烹也 - Alas, it's unjust to be cooked, an expression of Fang's perceived injustice.
  • 无故祟人，罪固当烹，何冤之有 - Without cause to harm others, the crime is indeed deserving of being cooked, why is there any injustice? A rhetorical question challenging Fang's claim.

  • 计甚恐怖 - The plan was extremely terrifying, indicating the intensity of the situation.
  • 复振叉作欲刺之势 - Again raised the fork, making a threatening gesture to stab, describing the threatening action.
  • 喝令速供 - Shouted to quickly provide, a command to expedite the response.

  • 于某年入京 - In a certain year entered the capital, specifying the time of Fang's arrival in the capital.
  • 因迫于饥寒，窃发人之冢 - Due to hunger and cold, secretly opened a tomb, describing Fang's motivation and action.
  • 为人所觉，群求擒捉 - Discovered by others, many sought to capture him, indicating the reaction to Fang's action.
  • 一时慞惶，用铁锹拒捕 - At once panicked, used a shovel to resist capture, describing Fang's immediate response to being discovered.
  • 希冀免脱 - Hoping to escape, expressing Fang's intention.
  • 不意连伤二人 - Unexpectedly injured two people, indicating the unintended consequences of Fang's actions.
  • 坐法当斩 - Convicted by law, should be executed, describing the legal punishment.
  • 今日大决，绑赴菜市，已临刑矣 - Today, the great execution, bound to be taken to the marketplace, already facing the death penalty, detailing the execution process.
  • 因极力挣扎，得脱身而走 - Due to vigorous struggle, managed to escape and run away, describing Fang's successful escape.
  • 方将逃避他所，讵意为此人拦阻 - Just about to escape to another place, unexpectedly blocked by this person, indicating the new obstacle.
  • 心实忿恨 - Heart truly filled with anger and resentment, expressing Fang's emotions.
  • 故与之较量 - Therefore, engaged in a contest with him, describing the confrontation.
  • 既老爷见责，焉敢迟留 - Since the master has rebuked, how dare I linger, a response to the authority's command.
  • 第乞秘密，小人他去 - Only ask for secrecy, the humble one will go elsewhere, a plea for privacy and a promise to leave.
  • 然则速去，勿触我怒 - Then quickly leave, do not provoke my anger, a final command to depart immediately.
  • 乃倚叉而坐 - Then leaned on the fork and sat down, describing the action after the confrontation.
  • 观者莫不骇异 - The observers were all shocked and amazed, describing the reaction of the audience.

  • 已枭示矣 - Means 'He had already been executed and displayed.'
  • 闻者咸叹异之 - Means 'Those who heard about it all marvelled at it.'
  • 愈神佟术 - Means 'The mystery surrounding Tong's abilities became even more profound.'
  • 持斋诵佛 - Means 'He maintained a vegetarian diet and recited Buddhist scriptures.'
  • 寡言喜睡 - Means 'He spoke little and enjoyed sleeping.'
  • 往往睡三四日不起 - Means 'He often slept for three or four days without waking up.'
  • 重门以内，无寸芥纤埃 - Means 'Within the inner doors, there was not a speck of dust.'
  • 一切箱匮几案，不见拂试而光洁可鉴 - Means 'All boxes, cabinets, tables, and desks were clean and polished without being wiped.'
  • 或言其有禁鬼之术 - Means 'Some said he had the ability to banish ghosts.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刑部 - Refers to the Ministry of Justice, one of the six ministries in the imperial bureaucracy responsible for criminal justice.
  • 持斋诵佛 - A practice associated with Buddhism, indicating a devout religious lifestyle.
  • 禁鬼之术 - Refers to the ability to control or banish ghosts, a concept rooted in Chinese folklore and religious beliefs.
  • 三年一更代 - A reference to the practice of changing servants or retainers periodically, which was common in noble households to maintain discipline and prevent corruption.

Proper Nouns

  • 刑部 - The Ministry of Justice.
  • 佟 - The name of the person described in the passage.
